common_issues: Bolting (heat >28C, long days, water stress); tip burn (Ca translocation failure under high VPD/fast growth); downy mildew (Hyaloperonospora, RH>90%); black rot (Xanthomonas, V-shaped lesions); Alternaria leaf spot; Pythium/Phytophthora root rot (warm reservoir, low DO); Erwinia/Pectobacterium soft rot; damping-off at seedling stage; diamondback moth (windowpane then holes); flea beetle shot-hole; aphids (virus vectors); cabbage looper, webworm, whitefly, thrips, leaf miner; nitrate accumulation; Ca-Mg-K antagonism; Mg deficiency; B deficiency (cracked petioles); Fe lockout from pH drift; algae in channels; heat-induced bitterness; uneven sizing from poor flow; root mat blocking NFT.
harvest_indicator: Ready 25-35 DAT; 8-12 fully expanded true leaves; rosette 20-25cm tall, 18-22cm wide; whole-plant fresh weight 150-300 g; petioles thick, crisp, pale green-white; deep green turgid leaves, no yellowing or tip burn; no flower stalk; harvest 5-7 AM.
tips: Sow 3-5mm deep in pre-soaked rockwool or peat plugs at 22-25C; expect 80-95% germination in 2-4 days under continuous moisture and 50-70% humidity.
Provide seedlings 14-16 hours of light at 150-200 umol/m2/s PPFD starting on day 2 to prevent leggy hypocotyls; keep root-zone temperature at 20-24C.
Begin nutrient feeding at EC 0.6-0.8 mS/cm (pH 5.8-6.2) once cotyledons fully open, then ramp to EC 1.2 by transplant to harden tissue against tip burn.
Transplant at 14-18 days when 2-3 true leaves have formed and roots protrude 2-3cm from the plug; spacing 15-20cm in NFT/DFT channels (about 25-30 plants/m2).
Maintain vegetative EC 1.4-1.8 mS/cm with a Ca:K ratio near 1:1.2 and supplemental Ca(NO3)2 at 150-180 ppm Ca to suppress inner-leaf tip burn common in PH lowland heat.
Hold dissolved oxygen above 6 mg/L and reservoir temperature below 26C; use chillers, shaded reservoirs, or night-only top-ups to prevent Pythium.
Target air temperature 18-24C day / 15-20C night; sustained >28C triggers bolting and bitter flavor, so deploy 30-50% shade net and evaporative cooling during dry season (March-May).
Run airflow at 0.3-0.5 m/s across the canopy with VPD 0.8-1.2 kPa to dampen downy mildew and improve calcium transpiration flow.

Scout twice weekly for diamondback moth (Plutella xylostella), flea beetle, and aphids; deploy yellow sticky traps at canopy height and rotate Bacillus thuringiensis kurstaki with spinosad to delay resistance.
Flush the system with plain pH-adjusted water (EC <= 0.4) for 24-48 hours before harvest to reduce nitrate accumulation, which is high in fast-grown Brassicas.
Harvest whole plant at 25-35 DAT in early morning (5-7 AM) when leaves are turgid and field heat is lowest; cut at the crown with sanitized blades to avoid Erwinia soft rot entry.
Pre-cool harvested heads to 2-4C within 1 hour and store at 0-2C with 95-98% RH for 14-21 days shelf life; never store with ethylene producers (tomato, banana) to avoid yellowing.

risks.title: Premature bolting
risks.description: Sustained temperatures above 28C combined with long photoperiods or moisture stress trigger reproductive transition, causing elongated stems, small bitter leaves, and flower stalk emergence. Symptoms include sudden vertical stem extension, narrowing of new leaves, and loss of crisp midrib texture.
risks.mitigation: Schedule cropping cycles to avoid March-May peak heat or use 30-50% shade net plus reservoir chilling to keep root-zone <= 24C. Select bolt-resistant cultivars such as Black Behi, Hybrid Black Summer, or Joi Choi F1 bred for tropical lowland conditions.

risks.title: Tip burn
risks.description: Localized calcium deficiency in rapidly expanding inner leaves causes necrotic browning along leaf margins even when nutrient solution Ca is adequate, because transpiration cannot deliver Ca to enclosed tissues. It typically appears 7-14 days before harvest under high light, low humidity, or fast EC growth.
risks.mitigation: Maintain 150-180 ppm Ca, keep VPD 0.8-1.2 kPa, and run gentle horizontal airflow across the inner canopy to drive Ca-bearing transpiration. Apply foliar 0.3% CaCl2 weekly during the last 14 days and avoid EC spikes >2.0.

risks.title: Downy mildew (Hyaloperonospora parasitica)
risks.description: Cool, humid mornings (>90% RH for >6 hr) allow oomycete sporulation, producing yellow angular lesions on the upper leaf surface with grey-violet sporangia underneath. Severe infections cause leaf collapse and unmarketable heads within 5-7 days.
risks.mitigation: Maintain RH 60-75%, increase canopy airflow to 0.3-0.5 m/s, and remove infected leaves immediately into sealed bags. Rotate preventive sprays of copper hydroxide (1.5 g/L) with Bacillus subtilis QST 713 or potassium phosphonate at 7-day intervals during wet season.

risks.title: Diamondback moth and flea beetle
risks.description: Plutella xylostella larvae create characteristic windowpane feeding then irregular holes, while flea beetles produce shot-hole damage on young leaves, both reducing marketable yield drastically. Populations explode in dry, warm Philippine conditions with overlapping cabbage crops nearby.
risks.mitigation: Enclose systems with 32-mesh insect netting, deploy yellow sticky traps at 1 per 4 m2, and rotate Bt kurstaki (1 g/L), spinosad (0.4 ml/L), and emamectin benzoate weekly to prevent resistance. Maintain a sanitation buffer free of cruciferous weeds (mustard, radish, wild bok choy) within 10 m.

risks.title: Pythium root rot
risks.description: Pythium aphanidermatum thrives when reservoir temperature exceeds 26C and DO drops below 5 mg/L, attacking root tips and producing brown, slimy, sloughing roots with rapid above-ground wilting under high light. Plants collapse within 48-72 hours of symptom onset.
risks.mitigation: Chill nutrient solution to 20-24C, maintain DO >= 6 mg/L via air stones or venturi injection, and dose hydrogen peroxide at 30-50 ppm or ionic copper-silver as a preventive. Sanitize channels between cycles with 1-2% sodium hypochlorite followed by thorough rinse.

risks.title: Nutrient imbalance (N excess and Ca-Mg antagonism)
risks.description: Over-reliance on ammoniacal N or excess K suppresses Ca and Mg uptake, causing interveinal chlorosis on older leaves, soft floppy growth, and elevated nitrate residues exceeding food-safety thresholds. Misreadings occur because EC remains in range while individual ion ratios drift.
risks.mitigation: Use >= 80% nitrate-N formulation, maintain K:Ca:Mg molar ratio near 1.2:1:0.4, and refresh reservoir fully every 10-14 days rather than topping up indefinitely. Run lab or ion-selective testing monthly and adjust stock A/B ratios accordingly.

risks.title: Heat stress and wilting
risks.description: Midday canopy temperature >32C with PPFD >500 umol/m2/s causes stomatal closure, transpiration collapse, and visible flagging of outer leaves; chronic exposure reduces head weight by 30-50%. Flavor turns bitter due to glucosinolate accumulation.
risks.mitigation: Install 30-50% white shade net, mist intermittently to drop leaf temperature 3-5C, and time peak growth periods to cooler months (November-February in Luzon lowlands). Elevate growing surfaces and insulate reservoirs to limit conductive heat gain.

growth_stages.description: Seeds absorb water, activate amylases, and rupture the seed coat as the radicle emerges. The hypocotyl hook begins to lift cotyledons; uniform moisture and temperature are critical to synchronize the cohort.
actions: Pre-soak rockwool/peat plugs in pH 5.8 water (EC 0.4)
Keep in darkness or low light <50 umol/m2/s
Avoid any nutrient solution until cotyledons emerge
Inspect daily for damping-off; remove failed plugs

growth_stages.description: Cotyledons fully open and turn green as photosynthesis begins; primary root extends 2-4cm. Seedlings are highly sensitive to overwatering and low light, which causes etiolation.
actions: Begin 14-16 h photoperiod at 150-200 umol/m2/s PPFD
Reduce humidity to 65-75% by venting dome
Start nutrient feed at EC 0.6-0.8, pH 5.8-6.2
Provide gentle airflow (0.2 m/s) to strengthen stems
Thin to 1 seedling per plug if doubled

growth_stages.description: First and second true leaves expand and the plant transitions to autotrophic growth. Root system branches out of the plug; stem thickens in preparation for transplant.
actions: Harden seedlings with airflow 0.3 m/s for 2 days pre-transplant
Scout for early aphid/flea beetle activity

growth_stages.description: Seedlings move into NFT/DFT channels or Kratky containers; roots adjust to flowing solution and new pH/EC environment. Brief transplant shock may cause leaf droop for 24-48 hours.
actions: Transplant at 2-3 true leaf stage, spacing 15-20cm
Set NFT flow 1-2 L/min per channel; DFT depth 3-5cm
Verify all roots contact solution film

growth_stages.description: Rapid leaf production and biomass accumulation; rosette diameter doubles weekly under good conditions. Highest nutrient and water demand of the cycle.
actions: Hold VPD 0.8-1.2 kPa for steady transpiration
Foliar Ca (0.3% CaCl2) weekly to prevent tip burn
Begin biweekly Bt or spinosad rotations for DBM

growth_stages.description: Inner leaves cup and thicken the petiole bases; fresh weight accumulates fastest in this window. Calcium translocation to inner leaves is the main physiological bottleneck.
actions: Keep canopy temp <= 26C; deploy shade if needed
Sustain horizontal airflow over inner canopy
Remove any necrotic outer leaves promptly
Final pest scout 5 days before harvest

growth_stages.description: Plant reaches marketable size (150-300 g, 20-25cm tall); nitrate flushing improves food safety and shelf life. Tissue firms slightly as growth slows.
actions: Switch to plain pH-adjusted water (EC <= 0.4) for 24-48 h
Maintain light and airflow as before
Inspect for tip burn, pest damage, soft rot
Pre-cool harvest tools and crates to 4C

growth_stages.description: Whole-plant cut at the crown; rapid field heat removal preserves turgor and chlorophyll. Tissue continues respiring, so cold chain integrity determines shelf life.
actions: Cut at crown with sanitized stainless blade
Pre-cool to 2-4C within 1 hour (hydrocool or forced air)
Pack in vented crates, max 2-3 layers
Target market within 7-14 days for premium quality

common_issues: Tip burn: localized Ca shortage in fast inner leaves from low transpiration (high RH, still air, dark head) and rapid growth at high EC/N/DLI, not low solution Ca. Bolting: mean temp >24C, long days, water/EC stress. Bitterness: heat >28C, over-maturity, EC spikes (lactucin/lactucopicrin). Bottom rot (Sclerotinia/Rhizoctonia): wet crowns, dense canopy. Downy mildew (Bremia lactucae): cool wet nights, RH>90%, leaf wetness >4h, race shifts to Bl:41EU. Pythium root rot: solution >24C, DO<5 mg/L. Fusarium wilt race 1, damping-off in wet/cold plugs. Aphids (Nasonovia) hidden in heads; leafminer (Liriomyza) mines; thrips + tospovirus; whitefly + sooty mold; fungus gnats; spider mites; slugs; LMV/big-vein viruses. Deficiencies: N pale old leaves; Ca tip burn; Mg interveinal old leaves; Fe interveinal new leaves at pH>6.3; B cracked midribs; Mo marginal scorch. EC drift, pH crash from nitrification, algae, root browning from low DO.
harvest_indicator: Ready 35-50 DAS (24-32 after transplant); upright cos head 20-30 cm tall, base 10-15 cm; whole-head 250-450 g; 18-28 leaves, 6-12 cupped firm inner leaves; light squeeze gives slight resistance; no seed-stalk at core; no bitter latex; cut 5-7 AM, cool to 1-2C in 30 min.
source: Cornell CEA Hydroponic Lettuce Handbook 2013; UF/IFAS HS1422; IBEB-EU Bremia race denominations (Bl:29-41EU); Rijk Zwaan/Bejo datasheets; Purdue CEA nutrition; web validation
tips: Romaine (cos) is the most heat- and bolt-prone lettuce type below 800 m; in PH lowlands pick proven warm-season cos cultivars such as Jericho (Israeli desert-bred), Coastal Star, Parris Island Cos, Cosbee, or Nevada, and shorten the cycle in the hot dry season.
Pre-chill romaine seed at 4-5C for 24-48 h before sowing to break thermo-dormancy; germination collapses above 25-28C media temperature, so hold plugs at 18-22C under a dome.

Sow one pelleted seed per pre-soaked rockwool/phenolic plug at pH 5.6-5.8, 3-4 mm deep; give 50 umol/m2/s PAR for the first 24 h then 200-250 umol/m2/s to keep seedlings compact (low light causes stretch, high light burns cotyledons).
Run seedlings at EC 0.8-1.0 mS/cm for the first 10-14 days, then transplant at the 3-4 true-leaf stage (day 14-18) once white roots emerge cleanly from the plug base.

Ramp EC stepwise above source-water EC: ~1.0 at transplant, 1.3-1.5 in early vegetative, and 1.6-1.8 mS/cm during head-fill; do not exceed ~2.0 in PH lowland heat to avoid osmotic-driven tip burn (Cornell ran 1150-1250 uS/cm above source for head lettuce).
Hold solution pH at 5.6-6.0, target 5.8; Ca/Mg uptake falls above pH 6.3 and Fe/Mn lock out below 5.4. Correct EC down with RO/clean water, never let the channel run dry.
Keep root-zone solution at 18-22C with a chiller or insulated/shaded reservoir; above 24-25C dissolved oxygen falls and Pythium pressure rises sharply.
Maintain DO at 7-8 mg/L (>4 mg/L minimum; crop failure below 3 mg/L per Cornell) using air stones, venturi, or a recirculating pond; aerate DFT and Kratky reservoirs.

Target a DLI of ~15-17 mol/m2/day (about 200-250 umol/m2/s for 16-18 h, or 250-350 for 14-16 h). Crucial Cornell finding: 17 mol/m2/day is only safe WITH downward/horizontal airflow over the canopy; without airflow tip burn limits you to ~12 mol/m2/day.

Run continuous gentle airflow of 0.3-0.5 m/s across and into the head center; airflow forces Ca-bearing transpiration into the youngest inner leaves and is the single most effective tip-burn control. A Ca-mobilizing biostimulant can give comparable control where fans are impractical.
Keep Ca at 120-180 ppm with a Ca:K ratio near 1:1.2 and B at 0.3-0.5 ppm; tip burn is a transport problem, so raising solution Ca alone will not fix it without airflow and controlled RH.
Under PH midday sun use 40-50% shade cloth to cap PPFD near 500-600 umol/m2/s and hold canopy under 26C; high green-light fraction has also been shown to lower romaine tip burn in plant factories.
Space 18-22 cm head-to-head (about 25-30 plants/m2 after re-spacing) so outer leaves expand and the canopy stays open for airflow and to limit Sclerotinia bottom rot.

Pre-harvest, drop EC to ~1.4-1.6 mS/cm for 3-5 days to lower nitrate and lift flavor; harvest whole heads at 5-7 AM when turgid, then hydro-cool or force-air cool to 1-2C within 30 min and store at 0-2C, 95-98% RH for 14-21 days shelf life.

risks.title: Tip burn (Ca translocation failure)
risks.description: Necrotic browning on the margins of the youngest inner leaves, caused by localized calcium deficiency in fast-expanding tissue rather than a shortage of Ca in solution. It is driven by low transpiration into the inner head (high humidity, still air, dark interior) and by rapid growth from high EC, high N, or high DLI; the cupped cos head is especially prone.
risks.mitigation: Run continuous horizontal airflow plus a fan directed into head centers (Cornell: airflow is what lets DLI go from ~12 to 17 mol/m2/day safely), hold VPD 0.8-1.2 kPa and RH 55-70%, cap EC at 1.8-2.0 during head-fill, and keep Ca 120-180 ppm with Ca:K near 1:1.2. Where fans are impractical, a Ca-mobilizing biostimulant gives comparable control.

risks.title: Bolting / premature seed stalk
risks.description: Romaine switches to reproductive growth when mean daily temperature exceeds ~24C for several days, especially under long photoperiods and water/EC stress, producing bitter latex and an elongated core. Once the apical meristem bolts the head is unsalvageable.
risks.mitigation: Use bolt-tolerant cos cultivars (Jericho, Coastal Star, Cosbee, Nevada), hold reservoir at 18-22C and canopy under 26C with 40-50% shade cloth, keep EC steady, and shorten the cycle to 30-35 DAS in hot months.

risks.title: Downy mildew (Bremia lactucae)
risks.description: Pale angular yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces with white-grey downy sporulation underneath, spreading fast in cool wet nights with leaf wetness over 4 h. Spores are airborne and races shift rapidly; the IBEB-EU has now denominated races up to Bl:41EU and commercial resistance claims refer to Bl:29EU and higher.
risks.mitigation: Grow varieties carrying current high-Bl resistance (check seed catalogs for Bl:29-41EU / Bl:38EU coverage, which breaks the older R58 gene), keep night RH below 85% with ventilation or dehumidification, avoid overhead irrigation and leaf wetness, and apply preventive Bacillus subtilis QST 713 or potassium bicarbonate in cool wet weeks.

risks.title: Pythium root rot (P. aphanidermatum, P. dissotocum)
risks.description: Brown, sloughing roots with loss of cortex, midday wilting despite full reservoirs, and stunted heads. Triggered by warm solution above 24C, low dissolved oxygen below 5 mg/L, and organic debris in the system.
risks.mitigation: Chill solution to 18-22C, hold DO above 6-7 mg/L with air stones or venturi injectors, filter return flow through 100-mesh, and flush/sanitize channels with 1-2% hydrogen peroxide between crops. Dose 30-50 ppm H2O2 in recirculation during high-risk heat.

risks.title: Bottom rot (Sclerotinia sclerotiorum / Rhizoctonia solani)
risks.description: Water-soaked brown lesions on lower leaves and crown, often with white cottony mycelium and black sclerotia, collapsing the head from below. Favored by dense canopies, prolonged leaf wetness, and lower leaves touching wet media or channel.
risks.mitigation: Keep channel slope 1:30-1:50 so no water pools at the crown, space heads 18-22 cm to keep the canopy open, remove infected plants plus surrounding plugs at once, and sanitize channels between cycles with 1-2% hydrogen peroxide or 200 ppm peracetic acid.

risks.title: Aphids and leafminers (Nasonovia ribisnigri, Liriomyza)
risks.description: Aphids colonize the head interior where sprays cannot reach and contaminate harvested product while vectoring viruses; Liriomyza larvae mine serpentine tunnels that render leaves unmarketable. Both worsen sooty mold and secondary disease.
risks.mitigation: Screen greenhouse intakes with 50-mesh netting, scout with yellow sticky traps weekly, release Aphidius colemani at 0.5-1 per m2 at first detection, and use Diglyphus isaea for leafminer or rotate spinosad and abamectin within label PHI. Prefer aphid-resistant (Nr-gene) cultivars where available.

risks.title: Bitterness from heat or over-maturity
risks.description: Romaine accumulates sesquiterpene lactones (lactucin, lactucopicrin) in its latex when stressed by heat above 28C, water deficit, or harvest past physiological maturity. Bitterness is irreversible once harvested and downgrades the crop.
risks.mitigation: Harvest within 3-5 days of reaching target weight, cut in the cool early morning, and keep EC steady and reservoirs cool in the final week. Provide shade and airflow during heat spells above 30C ambient, and drop EC to 1.4-1.6 mS/cm for the last 3-5 days.

growth_stages.description: Seed takes up water and gibberellin signaling triggers radicle emergence; romaine germinates best at 18-22C and is thermo-inhibited above 25-28C. Look for radicle protrusion by 36-48 h and cotyledon greening by day 3.
actions: Pre-soak rockwool/phenolic plugs in pH 5.8, EC ~0.5 mS/cm starter solution for 1 h
Sow one pelleted seed 3-4 mm deep per cell, cover tray with humidity dome
Hold media at 18-22C; pre-chill seed at 4-5C for 24-48 h if ambient is hot
Give 50 umol/m2/s PAR from hour 1 to prevent stretch
Do not feed beyond pre-soak nutrients this stage

growth_stages.description: Cotyledons fully expand and the first true leaf initiates as the seedling shifts from seed reserves to autotrophic growth. Watch for uniform greening, short stocky hypocotyls, and roots reaching the lower third of the plug.
actions: Remove humidity dome at day 3-4 to harden seedlings
Begin feeding at EC 0.8 mS/cm, pH 5.6-5.8 (N 150, K 200, Ca 120-150, Mg 45 ppm)
Raise light to 200-250 umol/m2/s for 16-18 h
Sub-irrigate to keep cotyledons dry and prevent damping-off
Run a small fan to thicken stems and harden tissue

growth_stages.description: Roots colonize the plug and leaf area expands rapidly during the photosynthetic ramp-up. Inspect for white healthy roots at the plug base and reject leggy or yellow seedlings.
actions: Apply preventive Bacillus subtilis or Trichoderma drench to plugs
Grade seedlings and cull runts before transplant
Begin scouting fungus gnats and aphids with sticky cards

growth_stages.description: Roots transition from plug to channel solution; growth briefly slows while new roots adapt to the nutrient film or aerated reservoir. Watch for root extension into the channel within 48-72 h and erect new leaves from the crown.
actions: Transplant at 3-4 true leaves once roots emerge from the plug
NFT flow 1-2 L/min/channel, film 2-3 mm; DFT 3-5 cm aerated; Kratky leave 2-3 cm air gap below netcup
Space 18-22 cm between plants, 25-30 cm rows (~25-30 plants/m2)
Keep DO 7-8 mg/L; aerate DFT/Kratky reservoirs
Shade new transplants 30% for the first 2 days
Verify channel slope (1:30-1:50) and no pooling at the base

growth_stages.description: Rapid leaf production and rosette expansion; the canopy closes between plants and biomass accumulation peaks. Look for a symmetric deep-green rosette with 8-16 expanded leaves.
actions: DLI 15-17 mol/m2/day; cap PPFD 500-600 umol/m2/s with 40-50% shade in PH midday
Run horizontal airflow 0.3-0.5 m/s over the canopy to prevent tip burn
Top up daily; full nutrient change every 7-10 days or when EC drift exceeds 0.4
Scout twice weekly for Bremia, aphids, leafminers
Hold DO 7-8 mg/L and root-zone under 22C

growth_stages.description: Inner leaves fold upright into the characteristic cos head; this is the highest tip-burn-risk window. Look for tightening central leaves and a base diameter reaching 10-14 cm.
actions: Hold EC 1.6-1.8 mS/cm; do not exceed ~2.0 in hot weeks
Keep Ca 120-180 ppm, Ca:K near 1:1.2, B 0.3-0.5 ppm
Direct gentle airflow into head centers to force inner-leaf transpiration
Keep night RH below 85% to limit Bremia and Sclerotinia
Remove lower leaves touching the channel surface

growth_stages.description: Head reaches target size and dry matter, sugars accumulate, and further growth risks bolting or bitterness. Check head firmness with light pressure and confirm tight upright inner leaves with no seed-stalk elongation.
actions: Drop EC to 1.4-1.6 mS/cm for 3-5 days to lower nitrate and improve flavor
Shade to ~50% and hold canopy under 24C
Withhold any pesticide within label PHI
Confirm head 250-450 g and base 10-15 cm
Schedule harvest 5-7 AM when leaves are most turgid
Sanitize knives and crates with 200 ppm chlorine or peracetic acid

growth_stages.description: Whole head is cut at the base and cooled immediately; field heat above 20C halves shelf life. Look for crisp upright leaves, no slimy base, and no latex browning at the cut.
actions: Cut at the crown with a single clean stroke; trim 1-2 outer wrapper leaves
Hydro-cool or force-air cool to 1-2C within 30 minutes
Pack in vented crates; avoid stacking pressure on heads
Target 14-21 days shelf life with an unbroken cold chain
Flush and sanitize channels with 1-2% H2O2 before the next cycle

common_issues: White rust (Albugo) white pustules on leaf underside; airflow + copper/metalaxyl. Leaf miner serpentine tunnels; spinosad, sticky traps, 32-mesh net. Flea beetle shotholes; neem + insect net. Pythium root rot from warm stagnant solution; airstones, nutrient temp <28C, H2O2 30 ppm. Hollow pithy stem from late harvest or low boron; cut by day 28, hold B 0.3-0.5 ppm. Leggy etiolated growth under low light; DLI >= 12, thin to 25-35 plants/m2. N-deficient yellowing of older leaves after ratoon; top up Ca(NO3)2+KNO3, NO3-N >= 180 ppm. Bolting in long photoperiod or heat; harvest before bud, shade 30% in dry season. Fe/Mn lockout above pH 6.8; daily pH check. Tip burn from Ca deficiency under high EC and low transpiration; Ca 150-180 ppm with airflow. Aphid/whitefly clusters on shoot tips; yellow traps, insecticidal soap. Damping-off at germination from saturated cool plugs.
harvest_indicator: First cut 28-30 days from sow (24-26 from transplant); ratoon cuts every 14-21 days. Stems 25-35 cm with 6+ internodes, diameter 4-6 mm, before flower buds. Cut 3-5 cm above crown leaving 2 nodes. Harvest 5-8 AM for max turgor. Plant yield 80-150 g per cut.
tips: Lightly nick or rub seeds against 220-grit sandpaper, then soak in 30-35C clean water for 18-24 hours (change water at 12 h) to lift germination from ~50% to >85%.
Hold media (rockwool/coco/peat plug) at 25-32C with constant moisture; radicle emerges in 2-4 days, true leaves by day 7. Below 22C germination stalls; above 35C causes seedling damping-off.
Use the upland/dryland type (narrow-leaf, erect) for NFT, DFT, and Kratky; reserve the aquatic/broadleaf type for deep-water raft systems where stems can sprawl on the water surface.

Practice cut-and-come-again: 4-6 successive cuts at 14-21 day intervals from one sowing, cutting 3-5 cm above the crown leaving 2 nodes for regrowth. Total productive cycle 60-90 days before lignification forces replant.
EC ramp: seedlings 0.6-0.8, vegetative 1.4-1.8, pre-harvest 1.6-2.0 mS/cm; after each ratoon cut drop EC by 0.2 for 48 h to favor soft regrowth, then ramp back. Keep N:K near 1.5:1.
Maintain pH 5.8-6.5; kangkong tolerates slight alkalinity (up to 7.0) better than lettuce but Fe/Mn lockout begins above 6.8. Check pH twice daily in shallow Kratky reservoirs.

Ratooning protocol: after cutting, immediately top up reservoir, foliar-mist Ca(NO3)2 at 0.5 g/L, and resume full EC within 24 h. Each successive ratoon yields ~80% of the previous; cull when stems exceed 8 mm or turn hollow.
Sow-to-first-harvest in PH lowland conditions: 24-30 days for upland type, 28-35 days for aquatic. Stagger weekly sowings for continuous supply.
Thrives at 25-35C ambient and tolerates 38C with adequate root-zone cooling (<28C nutrient temp); above 32C nutrient temp, DO drops below 5 mg/L, so add airstones or shade reservoir with 30% net.

Propagate by stem cuttings: take 15-20 cm cuttings with 3-4 nodes, strip lower leaves, direct-stick into net pots with rockwool collar; roots emerge in 4-6 days at 28C, shaving 10-14 days off cycle (dominant PH commercial practice).
Provide DLI >= 12 mol/m2/day; under PH dry-season sun (March-May) use 30% shade cloth to prevent leaf scorch and bolting. Indoor LED 200-250 umol/m2/s for 14-16 h photoperiod.

Scout twice weekly for leaf miner (Liriomyza), flea beetle, aphids, and whitefly; deploy yellow sticky traps and rotate neem oil 0.5%, spinosad, and Bt for lepidopteran larvae. Avoid systemic neonics within 14 days of harvest.

risks.title: White rust (Albugo ipomoeae)
risks.description: Chalky white blister-like pustules on leaf undersides with chlorotic yellow spots above; spreads rapidly under high humidity (>85%) and leaf wetness. Severe infection defoliates plants within 7-10 days and renders the crop unmarketable.
risks.mitigation: Increase canopy airflow, avoid overhead irrigation, and rogue infected leaves immediately into sealed bags. Apply copper hydroxide (2 g/L) or metalaxyl-mancozeb at first symptom; rotate kangkong out of affected channels for 2 cycles.

risks.title: Leaf miner and flea beetle damage
risks.description: Liriomyza larvae tunnel serpentine mines through leaf mesophyll while flea beetles create characteristic shothole feeding on tender leaves. Both reduce photosynthetic area and grade-out marketable leaves at retail.
risks.mitigation: Deploy yellow sticky traps at 1 trap per 4 m2 and cover seedling stages with 32-mesh insect net. Spray spinosad (0.4 mL/L) or abamectin on rotation; release Diglyphus isaea parasitoid in protected houses.

risks.title: Root rot in stagnant water (Pythium / Phytophthora)
risks.description: Brown, slimy, sloughing roots and sudden midday wilt despite full reservoirs, driven by nutrient temps >30C and DO <4 mg/L. Aquatic-type kangkong tolerates low O2 better than upland-type, but both succumb in stagnant warm Kratky setups.
risks.mitigation: Add airstones (>= 0.5 L/min per 10 L reservoir), shade or insulate tanks to keep nutrient solution <28C, and dose hydrogen peroxide at 30 ppm weekly or beneficial Trichoderma harzianum.

risks.title: Low light leading to leggy etiolated growth
risks.description: Under <10 mol/m2/day DLI or dense planting (>40 plants/m2), internodes elongate to 8-12 cm, stems become pale and weak, and bundles fail size grading. Common in roofed wet-season nurseries and over-shaded balconies.
risks.mitigation: Maintain DLI >= 12, thin to 25-35 plants/m2, and supplement with 150 umol/m2/s LED for 4 h dawn extension. Pinch growing tips at 4-leaf stage to force lateral branching.

risks.title: Hollow-stem disorder and lignification
risks.description: Late-cut or boron-deficient plants develop hollow, pithy main stems and tough fibrous bases that consumers reject. Often paired with cracked petioles and necrotic shoot tips.
risks.mitigation: Harvest by day 28-30 from sow (or day 18-21 from previous cut) before stem diameter exceeds 6 mm. Maintain B at 0.3-0.5 ppm and Ca at 150-180 ppm in solution.

risks.title: Nitrogen deficiency yellowing
risks.description: Uniform yellowing of older leaves progressing upward with reduced leaf size and stunted regrowth after ratooning; kangkong is a heavy N feeder (NO3-N demand 180-220 ppm). Common after the 2nd or 3rd cut when reservoir N is depleted.
risks.mitigation: Top up calcium nitrate plus potassium nitrate immediately after each cut, targeting NO3 >= 180 ppm. Monitor EC drift and do a full nutrient refresh every 14 days or after 3 ratoons.

risks.title: Regulated invasive species status
risks.description: Ipomoea aquatica is a federally listed noxious weed in the USA and restricted in several jurisdictions because escaped fragments root and clog waterways. In the Philippines it is native and unrestricted, but growers exporting transplants or operating near protected wetlands must contain runoff.
risks.mitigation: Filter all reservoir discharge through 1 mm screen and compost trimmings on-site; never dump nutrient solution containing stem fragments into canals or irrigation ditches. Confirm local DA/DENR rules before establishing systems near Laguna de Bay or other RAMSAR sites.

growth_stages.description: Hard-coated seeds are scarified and pre-soaked to break dormancy and synchronize germination. Without this step, germination drops below 50% and emergence is staggered over 2 weeks.
actions: Lightly abrade seeds with 220-grit sandpaper or nick coat with nail clipper
Soak in 30-35C clean water for 18-24 hours; change water at 12 h
Keep soaking vessel in shade at 28-30C ambient
Drain and surface-dry 30 min before sowing

growth_stages.description: Radicle emerges from scarified seed under warm, humid conditions in rockwool or coco plugs. Cotyledons unfold by day 4; this stage is most vulnerable to damping-off.
actions: Sow 1-2 seeds per plug at 5 mm depth
Maintain media temp 25-32C; humidity 80-90% under dome
Keep media moist with pH 5.8-6.2 plain water (no nutrients yet)
Provide low light 50-100 umol/m2/s once radicle visible
EC 0 (plain water) or 0.4 mS/cm dilute starter
Ventilate dome 10 min daily to prevent fungal bloom
Remove dome at full cotyledon expansion

growth_stages.description: First and second true leaves expand and the taproot extends into the plug. Plant becomes light-hungry and begins active nutrient uptake.
actions: Increase light to 150-200 umol/m2/s, 14 h photoperiod
Begin nutrient feed at EC 0.6-0.8 mS/cm, pH 5.8-6.2
Thin to 1 strongest seedling per plug

growth_stages.description: Plugs with 2-3 true leaves and visible roots are moved into NFT channels, DFT rafts, or Kratky pots. Spacing set for final canopy.
actions: Transplant when roots protrude >= 1 cm from plug
Shade 30% for 48 h post-transplant to reduce wilt
Verify net pot contact with nutrient film/water surface
Inspect for damping-off; remove failed plugs

growth_stages.description: Rapid stem elongation and leaf production; plant adds 2-3 nodes per week. Highest nutrient demand period; N and K uptake peaks.
actions: DLI 12-16 mol/m2/day; supplement LED if cloudy
Scout weekly for leaf miner, flea beetle, aphids
Top off reservoir daily; full refresh every 10-14 days
Pinch tip at 6-leaf stage to force lateral branching (optional)

growth_stages.description: Stems reach 25-35 cm and leaves attain full size and color. Sugar and dry matter accumulate; pre-flowering stage with no buds yet.
actions: Reduce nutrient temp toward 24-26C to firm tissue
Withhold any spray with PHI >3 days
Inspect for white rust pustules on leaf undersides
Confirm internode count >= 6, stem diameter 4-6 mm
Plan harvest in early AM (5-8 AM) for max turgor

growth_stages.description: Cut stems 3-5 cm above crown leaving 2 basal nodes for regrowth. Yields 80-150 g per plant; bundle and cool immediately to 4-10C.
actions: Harvest 5-8 AM before solar heat load
Use sanitized shears; cut above 2nd node from base
Bundle 250-500 g; rinse in 5 ppm chlorinated water
Cool to 4-10C within 2 h of cut
Top up reservoir; foliar mist Ca(NO3)2 at 0.5 g/L
Drop EC to 1.2 mS/cm for 48 h to favor soft regrowth
Inspect cut surfaces; remove any rotted basal stems

growth_stages.description: Lateral shoots emerge from retained nodes within 3-5 days; second cut ready in 14-21 days. Repeat for 4-6 cycles before lignification forces replant.
actions: Ramp EC back to 1.6-1.8 mS/cm within 72 h of cut
Maintain pH 5.8-6.5; refresh reservoir every 14 days
Watch for hollow-stem at base; replant if stem >8 mm
Cull plants showing white rust or root rot immediately
Track yield per cut; replant when output drops <60% of cut 1
Sanitize channels between full crop cycles

common_issues: Fusarium wilt; flowering reduces flavor
harvest_indicator: Pinch above node, before flowering
tips: Pinch flowering tips weekly to keep oils concentrated in leaves.
Propagate easily — root 10cm cuttings in plain water within 7 days.

risks.title: Fusarium wilt
risks.description: Sudden one-sided wilting and brown vascular streaks.
risks.mitigation: Use clean rockwool, sanitize reservoir, avoid water above 26C, rotate cultivars.

risks.title: Flowering / loss of flavor
risks.description: Flower spikes redirect energy from leaves.
risks.mitigation: Pinch flower buds twice a week; harvest stem tips regularly.

common_issues: BER (Ca), blight, whitefly
harvest_indicator: Fruit fully red, firm
tips: Hand-pollinate by gently shaking trusses each morning.
Prune to 1-2 leaders for Dutch Bucket; remove suckers weekly.
Drop EC 0.3 during fruit ripening to boost flavor.

risks.title: Blossom-end rot (BER)
risks.description: Sunken black patch at fruit base from calcium starvation.
risks.mitigation: Keep EC stable, supplement Cal-Mag, maintain even watering, humidity above 60%.

risks.title: Whitefly infestation
risks.description: Sap-sucking insects on leaf undersides; vector for viruses.
risks.mitigation: Yellow sticky traps; release Encarsia formosa; spray insecticidal soap.

risks.title: Late blight
risks.description: Brown lesions on leaves and fruit during humid spells.
risks.mitigation: Improve airflow, prune lower leaves, copper spray prophylactic.

common_issues: Inner-leaf tip burn from localized Ca deficiency at high EC/low airflow; bolting and bitterness above 25-28C; Pythium root/bottom rot in warm (>24C) low-oxygen solution; soft loose heads and leggy stretch under low light; aphids and leaf miners in the loose canopy; downy mildew (Bremia) and Sclerotinia/grey mould (Botrytis) at high humidity; nitrate accumulation under low light; Ca-K-Mg antagonism causing interveinal yellowing; algae on channels raising Pythium pressure; thrips vectoring tospovirus in PH lowland heat
harvest_indicator: Loose soft head 15-22cm wide with a blanched buttery yellow-green heart; outer wrapper leaves bright/medium green; whole-head weight 150-250g; firm-but-yielding feel when cupped; harvest before any central seed-stalk elongation or bitterness sets in
source: Cornell CEA Hydroponic Lettuce Handbook 2013; Purdue Ext HO-309-W; Hort Americas/e-gro Lettuce factsheet; Frontiers Plant Sci 2025 tipburn study; Produce Grower; Dizon Farms/Future Fresh PH retail
tips: Butterhead is a light feeder: ramp EC from 0.6 at seedling to 1.0 vegetative and hold 1.2-1.4 at finishing; in PH heat keep EC <=1.2 to limit tip burn.
Hold pH 5.6-6.2 (sweet spot 5.8); butterhead is sensitive to Fe/Ca lockout above pH 6.4, so dose pH-down little and often rather than in big swings.
Run nutrient with Ca:K near 1:2 and Ca:Mg near 2:1, and keep ammoniacal N <=15% of total N; this ratio plus Cal-Mag at 0.5g/L weekly is the single best tip-burn insurance.
Space transplants 18-22cm head-to-head (about 20-25 plants/m2 in NFT/DFT); crowding causes leggy stretch and shaded, slug-prone inner leaves.
Target DLI ~14-17 mol/m2/day; under LEDs give 200-250 umol/m2/s PPFD for 14-16h, and cap photoperiod at 14h in hot months to slow bolting.
Keep solution dissolved oxygen >=6 mg/L and water temp 18-22C; a chiller or shaded reservoir is the top priority in PH lowlands where ambient water drifts to 28-30C.
Run a constant gentle canopy airflow of 0.3-1.0 m/s (overhead/vertical fans) to drive transpiration and pull calcium into the youngest inner leaves.
For PH lowland heat pick bolt- and heat-tolerant butterhead types such as Buttercrunch, Manoa, or Carlo Rossa over delicate European Bibb strains.
Cold-prime seed before sowing in hot weather: imbibe 12-24h then refrigerate at 4-5C for 24-48h to beat thermo-inhibition and get even germination above 24C.
Germinate in the dark or dim light at 18-22C for 2-3 days, then move to full light immediately to prevent hypocotyl stretch.
Use 30-50% shade cloth in the PH dry season and aim for day temps 18-24C / nights 12-16C; sustained >25-28C drives bolting and bitterness.
For tip-burn-prone batches, briefly raise night-time air humidity to 80-90% (or run an overhead misting cycle) to ease transpiration stress on expanding leaves.
IPM: sticky traps plus weekly insecticidal soap or neem (azadirachtin ~0.5-1%) for aphids/leaf miners; sanitize channels between crops and exclude with insect netting against thrips.
Harvest whole heads in the cool early morning when fully turgid, cut at the crown with a sanitized blade, and pre-cool to 2-4C within an hour for shelf life.

risks.title: Inner-leaf tip burn
risks.description: Margins of the young inner butterhead leaves brown and die from localized calcium deficiency during rapid expansion, even when solution Ca is adequate, because low transpiration cannot deliver Ca to the fast-growing heart.
risks.mitigation: Hold EC <=1.2-1.4, keep Ca:K ~1:2 and Ca:Mg ~2:1, run constant 0.3-1.0 m/s canopy airflow, raise night humidity to 80-90%, supplement Cal-Mag 0.5g/L weekly, and avoid light/temperature spikes that outpace Ca uptake.

risks.title: Bolting and bitterness
risks.description: Sustained day temperatures above 25-28C (common in PH lowlands) trigger seed-stalk elongation; leaves turn bitter and milky and heads fail to fill.
risks.mitigation: Use heat-tolerant varieties (Buttercrunch, Manoa, Carlo Rossa), apply 30-50% shade cloth, chill solution to 18-22C, cap photoperiod at 14h, and harvest by day 35-40 in hot months before stalks appear.

risks.title: Pythium root and bottom rot
risks.description: Warm (>24C), low-oxygen nutrient solution favors Pythium water mold, causing brown slimy roots, wilting, and basal/bottom rot of the loose head; spreads fast through recirculating water.
risks.mitigation: Chill solution to 18-22C, keep DO >=6 mg/L, shade and cover reservoirs to block algae, sanitize channels and tools between crops, and consider beneficial Bacillus/Trichoderma or low-dose H2O2 in recirculating water.

risks.title: Leggy stretch and soft loose heads
risks.description: Low light (DLI <12), high night temperature, or overcrowding produce pale, elongated, poorly hearted heads that do not form the buttery blanched center buyers expect.
risks.mitigation: Provide DLI 14-17 mol/m2/day at 200-250 umol/m2/s for 14-16h, drop night temps to 12-16C, and keep spacing at 18-22cm (20-25 plants/m2).

risks.title: Aphids and leaf miners in the canopy
risks.description: The loose, layered butterhead canopy shelters aphids and leaf-miner larvae that contaminate the eating leaves and vector viruses; hard to wash out of a soft head.
risks.mitigation: Scout with yellow sticky traps, exclude with insect netting, release lacewing/ladybird predators where feasible, and spot-treat with insecticidal soap or neem (azadirachtin 0.5-1%) on a 5-7 day rotation.

risks.title: Foliar disease at high humidity (downy mildew, grey mould)
risks.description: Persistent leaf wetness and humidity above 85% with poor airflow favor Bremia lactucae (downy mildew) and Botrytis/Sclerotinia, rotting outer wrapper leaves and head bases in the humid PH climate.
risks.mitigation: Maintain daytime humidity 55-70%, keep steady airflow, avoid wetting foliage, remove infected leaves promptly, and choose downy-mildew-resistant (Bremia Bl-coded) varieties.

growth_stages.description: Cold-prime seed for hot weather, then sow one pelleted/raw seed per rockwool or phenolic plug 3-5mm deep.
actions: Imbibe seed 12-24h then chill at 4-5C for 24-48h to beat thermo-inhibition
Sow 1 seed per plug, cover lightly with vermiculite
Hold 18-22C, plain water or EC <=0.4, no strong nutrients

growth_stages.description: Radicle and cotyledons emerge; keep cool and just-moist to avoid damping-off.
actions: Germinate in dark/dim light at 18-22C for 2-3 days
Move to full light as soon as cotyledons open to prevent stretch
Keep plugs moist not saturated; ensure airflow
Begin very dilute feed at EC 0.4-0.6

growth_stages.description: First 2-3 true leaves develop; root system establishes in the plug.
Move plugs into NFT/DFT channels or Kratky vessels at final spacing; roots reach the nutrient film.
Rapid rosette growth and aggressive leaf expansion; highest tip-burn risk window.
actions: Keep Ca:K ~1:2, Ca:Mg ~2:1; add Cal-Mag 0.5g/L weekly
Hold day 18-24C, raise night humidity to 80-90% if tip burn appears

growth_stages.description: Inner leaves cup and blanch into the buttery soft heart characteristic of butterhead.
actions: Keep day 18-24C / night 12-16C to encourage hearting
Sustain constant airflow and DO >=6 mg/L
Scout for bolting and tip burn; apply 30-50% shade if hot

growth_stages.description: Loose soft head 15-22cm wide with a blanched buttery heart, 150-250g whole-head weight.
actions: Cut at crown with a sanitized blade in the cool early morning
Harvest before any seed-stalk elongation or bitterness
Pre-cool to 2-4C within 1 hour of cut
Keep roots/wrapper intact for live-sale to extend shelf life

common_issues: Flea beetles; bolting
harvest_indicator: Leaves 20-25cm
tips: Cut yellow-flowering stems immediately to delay bolting.
Can use cut-and-come-again harvesting by removing outer 30% of leaves.

risks.title: Bolting
risks.description: Premature flowering and bitter taste from heat stress.
risks.mitigation: Keep temperatures below 26C and monitor system pumps.

common_issues: BER; aphids; slow early growth
harvest_indicator: Full-size, color change
tips: Allow first king flower to set then prune subsequent buds for stronger plant.
Trellis with Florida weave to support heavy fruit load.

risks.title: Blossom-end rot
risks.description: Black sunken spot opposite stem from Ca uptake failure.
risks.mitigation: Stable EC, weekly Cal-Mag, humidity 60-75%.

common_issues: Powdery mildew; spider mites
harvest_indicator: Fruit 18-22cm, firm
tips: Choose parthenocarpic varieties — no pollination needed.
Train to single-stem umbrella system for max yield per sqm.

risks.title: Calcium/Potassium deficiency
risks.description: Scorched leaf edges or poor fruit set.
risks.mitigation: Supplement calcium and maintain proper EC during fruiting.

risks.title: Powdery mildew
risks.description: White powdery spots on leaves.
risks.mitigation: Ensure good airflow and maintain humidity below 80%.

common_issues: Root rot; poor fruit set in heat
harvest_indicator: Full red color
tips: Pinch off early flowers during vegetative stage to build stronger plants.
Use day-neutral varieties like Albion or Seascape for year-round yield.
Requires manual pollination by shaking or using a soft brush.

risks.title: Tip Burn
risks.description: Calcium deficiency from low humidity.
risks.mitigation: Keep humidity above 60%.

risks.title: Root Rot
risks.description: Browning roots due to poor oxygenation.
risks.mitigation: Use air stones and maintain water temp below 20C.

common_issues: Pythium root rot; sensitive to high temperature
harvest_indicator: Leaves 10-15cm, cut-and-come-again
tips: Keep nutrient solution cool (under 22C) to prevent Pythium root rot.
Requires cooler ambient temperature to prevent early bolting.

risks.title: Pythium root rot
risks.description: Brown slimy roots in warm water.
risks.mitigation: Keep reservoir cool, add hydrogen peroxide if needed.

common_issues: Invasive root systems in shared channels
harvest_indicator: Frequent pruning of tips
tips: Extremely fast growing and roots can easily clog NFT channels if not pruned.
Harvest regularly to promote bushier growth.

risks.title: System clogging
risks.description: Mint roots grow aggressively and can block water flow.
risks.mitigation: Regularly trim roots or grow in individual Dutch buckets.

actions: Place in water until roots form

common_issues: Aphids; high temperatures cause bitterness
harvest_indicator: Leaves are large enough to eat
tips: Use 'cut-and-come-again' harvesting.
Tolerates wider EC range.

risks.title: Aphids
risks.description: Small insects on underside of leaves.
risks.mitigation: Use sticky traps or neem oil.

common_issues: Bolting in high heat
harvest_indicator: Compact heads form
tips: Sensitive to high light/heat causing bolting.
Harvest whole head.

risks.title: Bolting
risks.description: Premature flowering.
risks.mitigation: Keep temps below 24C.

common_issues: Nutrient burn if EC too high
harvest_indicator: Leaves 5-10cm long
tips: Harvests very quickly in 3-4 weeks.
Sensitive to high salt concentrations.

risks.title: Nutrient Burn
risks.description: Yellowing/browning leaf tips.
risks.mitigation: Keep EC below 1.4.

common_issues: Early bolting; slow germination
harvest_indicator: Leaves 10-15cm long
tips: Crack seeds gently before planting.
Use slow-bolt varieties.

risks.title: Bolting
risks.description: Flowers early in heat.
risks.mitigation: Keep temperatures cool.

common_issues: Root rot from stagnant water
harvest_indicator: Stems 15cm long
tips: Hardy and compact.
Propagate from cuttings for faster results.

risks.title: Root Rot
risks.description: Roots drown in stagnant water.
risks.mitigation: Ensure good aeration.

common_issues: Root rot
harvest_indicator: Stems 10-15cm long
tips: Prune regularly to prevent woody stems.
Harvest before flowers open.

risks.title: Root Rot
risks.description: Soggy roots.
risks.mitigation: Avoid constant soaking if possible.

common_issues: Overcrowding in net cups
harvest_indicator: Leaves 15-20cm tall
tips: Trim back to 2-3cm above crown every few weeks.
Very productive and easy.

risks.title: Overcrowding
risks.description: Clumps grow too thick.
risks.mitigation: Divide mature clumps occasionally.

common_issues: Spider mites; heavy fruit breaking branches
harvest_indicator: Skin is glossy and firm
tips: Requires robust trellising.
Manual pollination needed indoors.

risks.title: Branch Breakage
risks.description: Fruits are heavy.
risks.mitigation: Use stakes or cages.

common_issues: Powdery mildew; poor pollination
harvest_indicator: Fruit 15-20cm long
tips: Requires significant space.
Must hand pollinate female flowers using male flowers.

risks.title: Powdery Mildew
risks.description: White spots on massive leaves.
risks.mitigation: Good airflow and prune old leaves.

common_issues: Root rot if water is stagnant
harvest_indicator: Stems are 15cm long
tips: Highly oxygenated water is critical.
Cut leaving stubble for regrowth.

risks.title: Stagnant Water
risks.description: Causes root rot and attracts pests.
risks.mitigation: Use strong air stones.

common_issues: Root rot if water is too warm
harvest_indicator: Outer leaves are large enough
tips: Harvest outer leaves for cut-and-come-again yield.
Keep roots below 24C.

risks.title: Root Rot
risks.description: High reservoir temps lead to rot.
risks.mitigation: Keep water cool.

common_issues: Crown rot; slow early growth
harvest_indicator: Stalks are thick and firm
tips: Keep basal plate above waterline to avoid crown rot.
Requires patience as it is a slow grower.

risks.title: Crown Rot
risks.description: Base rots if submerged.
risks.mitigation: Keep base dry.

common_issues: Poor pollination without airflow
harvest_indicator: Pods are tender and seeds not bulging
tips: Topping young plants encourages bushier growth.
Shake plants gently to aid pollination indoors.

risks.title: Poor Yield
risks.description: Lack of pollination.
risks.mitigation: Use fans or shake plants.

common_issues: Aphids; blossom drop from heat/stress
harvest_indicator: Color change and firm flesh
tips: Needs high EC during fruiting for heat and yield.
Use heat mat for germination.

risks.title: Blossom Drop
risks.description: Flowers fall off without setting fruit.
risks.mitigation: Ensure stable temp and hand pollinate.

common_issues: Root rot from overwatering
harvest_indicator: Stems are 10-15cm
tips: Best started from cuttings.
Prefers slightly drier roots; ensure excellent aeration.

risks.title: Root Rot
risks.description: Roots drown easily.
risks.mitigation: Avoid constant soaking.

common_issues: Slow early growth
harvest_indicator: Leaves are large enough to pick
tips: Resilient but needs consistent nutrients.
Harvest before flowering.

risks.title: Mildew
risks.description: Fungal issues in high humidity.
risks.mitigation: Ensure good airflow.

common_issues: Slow seed germination
harvest_indicator: Stems are 10-15cm tall
tips: Very beginner-friendly.
Responds well to cut-and-come-again.

risks.title: Slow germination
risks.description: Seeds take up to 3 weeks.
risks.mitigation: Soak seeds overnight.

common_issues: Vines breaking under fruit weight; powdery mildew
harvest_indicator: Fruit slips easily from vine; sweet aroma
tips: Crucial to use slings to support fruit.
Needs manual pollination.

risks.title: Vine Stress
risks.description: Heavy fruit breaks vine.
risks.mitigation: Use hammocks/slings for fruit.

common_issues: Bolting in heat; aphids
harvest_indicator: Head is tight and green
tips: Heavy feeder, requires high EC.
Keep temperatures low to avoid bitter bolting.

risks.title: Bolting
risks.description: Premature flowering from heat.
risks.mitigation: Keep ambient temps below 24C.

common_issues: Head cracking from uneven watering
harvest_indicator: Head is solid and firm to touch
tips: Requires significant space.
Heavy feeder that depletes water quickly.

risks.title: Head Cracking
risks.description: Sudden influx of water causes splitting.
risks.mitigation: Keep EC and water levels very consistent.

common_issues: Bolting in heat; flea beetles; clubroot
harvest_indicator: Stems pencil-thick, first flower buds appear
tips: Harvest when first flower buds form for tenderest stems.
Cut central stem first then side shoots regrow for second harvest.
Variety Karillo (Rijk Zwaan) tolerates Philippine lowland heat better than imports.

risks.title: Bolting
risks.description: Premature flowering above 26C produces tough fibrous stems.
risks.mitigation: Keep solution below 22C; harvest at first bud sign; provide 30% shade cloth.

risks.title: Flea beetles
risks.description: Tiny holes peppering young leaves.
risks.mitigation: Floating row cover early stage; yellow sticky traps; neem oil weekly.

growth_stages.description: Move to channel when 4 true leaves.

common_issues: BER; cracking on irregular watering; whitefly
harvest_indicator: Fruit fully red, oblong, firm
source: Rijk Zwaan Solarino datasheet, NoSoilSolutions, Web validation
tips: Plum/paste varieties (e.g. Solarino, San Marzano) have less water content — ideal for sauces.
Maintain steady irrigation to prevent radial cracking.
Drop EC by 0.3 during ripening to concentrate sugars.

risks.title: Blossom-end rot (BER)
risks.description: Plum types especially susceptible due to elongated fruit shape and Ca uptake bottleneck.
risks.mitigation: Stable EC, weekly Cal-Mag supplement, keep humidity 60-70%.

risks.title: Fruit cracking
risks.description: Concentric or radial cracks from sudden EC/water swings.
risks.mitigation: Keep fertigation cycles consistent; avoid drought-then-flood pattern.

common_issues: BER; aphids; thin fruit walls if low Ca
harvest_indicator: Long horn-shape (15-20cm), fully red/yellow, glossy
source: Rijk Zwaan Palermo datasheet, IGWorks, NoSoilSolutions
tips: Palermo-type horns ripen faster than blocky bell peppers — earlier red color = sweeter flavor.
Bag clusters with insect mesh during flowering to keep clean fruit.
Pinch first king flower to build stronger bush.

risks.title: Blossom-end rot
risks.description: Elongated fruit prone to Ca starvation at distal tip.
risks.mitigation: Stable EC, weekly Cal-Mag, humidity 60-75%, even fertigation.

risks.title: Aphid colonies
risks.description: Curling new growth, sticky honeydew, sooty mold.
risks.mitigation: Yellow sticky traps; release ladybugs; spray insecticidal soap.

common_issues: Tip burn (inner leaf-margin browning from local Ca shortage at high EC/low airflow/high humidity); bolting and bitterness above 26-27C in PH lowlands; bottom/lettuce drop rot (Pythium, Rhizoctonia, Sclerotinia) in saturated media or warm solution; downy mildew (Bremia lactucae) and powdery mildew trapped in lobed, water-holding leaves; root rot from warm (>24C) low-DO solution; aphids, leaf-miner, thrips, and whitefly in screenhouses; nutrient burn and soft, perishable leaves; manganese/calcium tip-curl; mechanical bruising of tender loose leaves during harvest and packing
harvest_indicator: Open loose rosette of lobed oakleaf leaves 15-20cm long, full leaf count, leaves crisp and turgid; green forms bright/glossy, red forms with full anthocyanin color; whole-head 120-220g at 28-42 days; harvest before any central stem elongation (bolting)
source: Rijk Zwaan Salanova/Cousteau RZ oakleaf datasheet, Cornell CEA Lettuce Handbook, UF/IFAS HS1422, Produce Grower leafy-greens guide, Howard Resh, PH market price surveys
tips: Oakleaf is a tender loose-leaf type (e.g. Salanova Cousteau RZ green, red oakleaf forms) bred for hydroponics: harvest whole-head or single-cut-at-base for ready-to-eat baby leaves.
Run a 3-stage EC ramp: 0.6 mS/cm in the nursery, 1.0 mS/cm after transplant, 1.2-1.4 mS/cm to maturity; hold the full range 0.8-1.6 and cap at 1.6 in hot months to limit tip burn.
Keep pH 5.5-6.0 for best Ca/Mg/Fe uptake; oakleaf's high N and Ca demand makes drift above 6.2 a fast route to interveinal yellowing and tip burn.
Target DLI 12-15 mol/m2/day (PPFD ~180-250 umol/m2/s for 14-16h); going above ~17 pushes growth faster than Ca can move into new leaves and triggers tip burn on this tender type.
Spacing: start seedlings dense then respace to final 18-22cm head-to-head, roughly 30-40 plants/m2 in NFT/DFT, so the spreading lobed rosette gets airflow and light.
Hold solution/root-zone temp 18-22C with a chiller in PH lowlands; warm (>24C) low-oxygen water is the main cause of Pythium root rot and bolting.
Maintain dissolved oxygen 6-8 mg/L (air stones in DFT/Kratky, good film in NFT) for white, branching roots and faster bulking.
Lobed oakleaf leaves trap free water in their crevices, so run gentle constant horizontal airflow over the canopy and keep humidity 55-70% to dry leaves and beat downy/powdery mildew and tip burn.
In the dry season use 30-40% shade cloth and limit growlights to 12-14h once day temps climb to keep the canopy under ~26C and delay bolting.
Cut-and-come-again works on the loose-leaf forms: harvest outer leaves and the plant reflushes 2-3 cycles, but whole-head single-cut gives the cleanest Salanova-style baby-leaf yield.
Transplant from nursery at day 10-14 (2-3 true leaves, roots through the plug) into the main channel at EC 1.0, pH 5.8 to avoid transplant check.
For IPM in screenhouses scout weekly and spot-treat aphids/thrips with insecticidal soap or 1-2% neem oil; release/encourage natural enemies rather than blanket spraying tender leaves.
Harvest at first light when leaves are fully turgid and cool to ~4C within an hour; oakleaf is very perishable and bruises easily, so handle by the base, never compress the rosette.
Sow fresh, primed seed and germinate at 18-20C; oakleaf/lettuce seed goes thermo-dormant above ~25C, so pre-chill or sow in the coolest part of the nursery in PH heat.

risks.title: Tip burn
risks.description: Inner and new-leaf margins brown from localized calcium deficiency when leaves expand faster than Ca can be moved to them, driven by high EC, high humidity, low airflow, or high DLI.
risks.mitigation: Hold EC <=1.6, keep humidity 55-70%, run constant gentle airflow across the canopy, keep DLI ~12-15, and supplement Cal-Mag (~0.5 g/L) weekly during rapid growth.

risks.title: Bolting and bitterness
risks.description: Day temps above 26-27C (common in PH lowlands) trigger central stem elongation; leaves turn bitter and milky and the head loses market value.
risks.mitigation: Hold day temp under 24C with shade cloth 30-40% and a chiller, choose bolt-tolerant oakleaf cultivars, shorten the cycle, and harvest by day 35-40 in the hot season.

risks.title: Pythium / root rot
risks.description: Warm (>24C), low-oxygen nutrient solution lets Pythium and other water molds attack roots; roots turn brown and slimy and plants wilt midday and stunt.
risks.mitigation: Chill solution to 18-22C, keep DO 6-8 mg/L, sanitize channels/reservoir between crops, avoid overcrowding, and consider beneficial microbes or food-grade H2O2 dosing.

risks.title: Downy and powdery mildew
risks.description: Bremia lactucae (downy) and powdery mildew thrive in the humid PH climate and in the water-holding crevices of lobed oakleaf leaves, causing angular yellow patches or white coating.
risks.mitigation: Lower humidity to 55-70%, increase canopy airflow and plant spacing, avoid wetting foliage, remove infected leaves, and use resistant cultivars; spot-treat with approved biofungicides.

risks.title: Lettuce drop / bottom rot
risks.description: Sclerotinia and Rhizoctonia rot the crown and lower leaves where they sit in saturated media or stagnant film, collapsing the plant near harvest.
risks.mitigation: Avoid waterlogging the crown, keep media/film free-draining and aerated, sanitize between cycles, and harvest promptly once heads mature.

risks.title: Sucking pests in screenhouses
risks.description: Aphids, thrips, leaf-miner, and whitefly colonize tender oakleaf leaves, deform growth, and vector viruses; they hide in the lobed leaf folds.
risks.mitigation: Use fine insect netting, sticky traps, and weekly scouting; spot-treat with insecticidal soap or 1-2% neem and conserve natural enemies instead of broad sprays on edible leaves.

growth_stages.description: Sow 1 primed/pelleted oakleaf seed per pre-soaked rockwool or phenolic plug, ~3mm deep.
actions: Keep below 25C to avoid seed thermo-dormancy

growth_stages.description: Radicle and cotyledons emerge; lettuce germinates fastest in cool, moist conditions.
actions: Mist to keep plug moist not soggy

growth_stages.description: First true leaves form in the nursery channel before transplant.
Move 2-3 true-leaf seedlings with roots through the plug into the main NFT/DFT channel.
actions: Shade lightly 1-2 days to limit transplant check

growth_stages.description: Roots colonize the channel and the rosette begins active expansion.
Lobed oakleaf rosette spreads and gains weight; peak nutrient and Ca demand.
actions: Cal-Mag ~0.5 g/L weekly to prevent tip burn

growth_stages.description: Full-size open rosette with full leaf count and color; head firms up.
actions: Hold EC 1.2-1.4, monitor for tip burn
Inspect for mildew and aphids in leaf folds

growth_stages.description: Cut whole-head at base (Salanova single-cut) or pluck outer leaves for cut-and-come-again at 120-220g.
actions: Single-cut at crown with sanitized blade at first light
Or pluck outer leaves for 2-3 reflush cycles
Cool to ~4C within 1 hour, handle by base to avoid bruising

common_issues: Tip burn from poor Ca transport at high EC/low airflow; bolting and leaf bitterness when day temps exceed 28C (Batavia tolerates higher than butterhead but still bolts in prolonged heat); Pythium/root rot in reservoirs above 24C; downy mildew (Bremia) and damping-off in high humidity; bottom rot (Sclerotinia/Rhizoctonia) on crowns sitting in saturated media; aphids and thrips in screenhouses; lettuce mosaic virus; nitrogen/calcium deficiency on fast growth; algae in light-exposed channels
harvest_indicator: Firm semi-heading rosette of crinkled, glossy crisp leaves 20-28cm across; dense sweet heart; whole-head weight 250-350g; cut before any seed-stalk elongation or leaf bittering; 35-50 days from sow in lowland heat
source: Rijk Zwaan lettuce assortment (Batavia: Olmetie/Jonction/Meditation RZ), Cornell CEA Lettuce Handbook, Frontiers 2025 tipburn study, Howard Resh, DA-PH price monitoring
tips: Batavia (French/summer crisp) is the most heat- and bolt-tolerant common lettuce type, bridging crisphead and loose-leaf; it is the safest choice for Philippine lowland CEA where butterhead and romaine bolt early.
Pick a proven hydroponic Batavia: Olmetie RZ (dense fast green head), Jonction (slow-bolt, warm-climate), or Meditation RZ (good shelf life) all run well in NFT/DFT year-round.
Ramp EC by stage: 0.6 mS/cm in the nursery, 1.0-1.2 at transplant, then 1.4-1.6 to maturity; cap EC at 1.6-1.8 in hot months because high salts plus heat worsen tip burn.
Hold pH 5.5-6.2 (target 5.8) for balanced Ca, Mg and micronutrient uptake; correct with phosphoric/potassium hydroxide and check daily as warm roots shift pH fast.
Even though Batavia tolerates heat, keep day air 18-24C and chill the nutrient solution to 18-22C; a reservoir above 24C invites Pythium and triggers bolting past day 40.
Insulate and shade reservoirs, run a chiller or buried sump, and keep dissolved oxygen at 6-8 mg/L with an air stone or NFT film to protect roots in tropical heat.
Target DLI 15-17 mol/m2/d at canopy and PPFD 250-300 umol/m2/s (max ~400); 14-16h photoperiod indoors. Excess light plus heat speeds growth faster than Ca can be delivered, causing tip burn.
Run gentle constant downward airflow (vertical fans) over the canopy: it boosts transpiration and pulls calcium to leaf margins, the single most effective tip-burn control in CEA lettuce.
Keep relative humidity 55-70% in the day; allowing higher humidity at night (>85%) actually aids Ca mobility to the meristem and further reduces tip burn.
Space heads 18-22cm apart (about 20-25 plants/m2 in NFT/DFT) so the crinkled outer leaves expand fully without crowding, trapped moisture, or mildew.
Use a 2-3 week nursery on a low-EC nursery channel, then transplant 1 plug per net cup at 3-4 true leaves; transplanting young keeps the crop uniform and on a 35-50 day cycle.
For IPM in screenhouses use yellow sticky traps plus spot Bacillus thuringiensis or insecticidal soap/neem for aphids and thrips; physically exclude vectors to limit lettuce mosaic virus.
Sanitize channels and reservoirs between batches (H2O2 ~1 ml/L of 3% or peroxyacetic dip) and avoid recirculating untreated water to break Pythium and Bremia cycles.

Harvest in the cool early morning when leaves are fully turgid, cut at the crown with a sanitized blade, and cool to 2-4C within an hour; Batavia's crisp leaves give a noticeably longer shelf life than butterhead.

risks.title: Tip burn
risks.description: Inner/young leaf margins brown and die from localized calcium deficiency when rapid growth (high light, heat, high EC) outpaces Ca transport, especially with weak transpiration.
risks.mitigation: Hold EC 1.4-1.6, run constant downward airflow over the canopy, keep day RH 55-70% and night RH high, and supplement Cal-Mag (~0.5 g/L) weekly during fast growth.

risks.title: Bolting and bitterness
risks.description: Prolonged day temps above 28C and long heat spells push even bolt-tolerant Batavia to elongate a seed stalk; leaves turn bitter and milky and lose market value.
risks.mitigation: Use 30-40% shade cloth in the dry season, chill solution to 18-22C, choose slow-bolt cultivars (Jonction/Meditation RZ), and harvest by day 45-50 in hot months.

risks.title: Pythium root rot
risks.description: Roots turn brown, slimy and sloughed when the nutrient solution warms above 24C and dissolved oxygen drops, a constant threat in tropical lowland reservoirs.
risks.mitigation: Chill solution below 22C, insulate and shade tanks, keep DO 6-8 mg/L, sanitize between batches, and dose H2O2 ~1 ml/L weekly or use beneficial microbes.

risks.title: Downy mildew (Bremia lactucae)
risks.description: Pale-yellow angular blotches on upper leaves with white downy growth beneath, spreading fast under high humidity, dense canopies and leaf wetness.
risks.mitigation: Improve spacing and airflow, keep RH out of saturation by day, avoid wetting foliage, remove infected leaves, and select Bremia-resistant Batavia genetics.

risks.title: Bottom/crown rot
risks.description: Sclerotinia or Rhizoctonia softens the lower crown and outer leaves when bases sit in saturated, poorly aerated media or stagnant film.
risks.mitigation: Keep net cups above the solution line, ensure free drainage and steady NFT film, sanitize media and channels, and avoid over-deep planting.

risks.title: Aphids, thrips and lettuce mosaic virus
risks.description: Sap-sucking aphids and thrips colonize heads in warm screenhouses, deform leaves, and transmit lettuce mosaic and other viruses that stunt and mottle the crop.
risks.mitigation: Exclude with fine insect netting, scout weekly with yellow sticky traps, spot-treat with insecticidal soap or neem, and remove and discard virus-symptomatic plants immediately.

growth_stages.description: Sow 1-2 pelleted seeds per rockwool/phenolic plug, 3mm deep, for nursery germination.
Cotyledons emerge; Batavia germinates well in warmth but avoid heat-induced dormancy above 28C.
True leaves form on the nursery channel; build a strong root plug before transplant.
Move 3-4 true-leaf plugs to the main NFT/DFT channel at final spacing.
actions: Seat net cups above the film, not submerged

growth_stages.description: Rosette expands and leaves begin to crinkle; calcium demand rises with growth rate.
Semi-heading Batavia rosette firms up into a dense crisp heart.
actions: Keep RH 55-70% day, higher at night

growth_stages.description: Head reaches target size; hold conditions and watch for any bolting in hot spells.
actions: Scout for tip burn and mildew
Plan a cool morning cut window

growth_stages.description: Single cut at the base when the crinkled head is firm, 20-28cm across and 250-350g.
actions: Cut at crown with sanitized blade in early morning
Bag dry whole heads for long shelf life

common_issues: Inner-leaf tip burn from localized Ca deficiency at high EC/low transpiration; bolting and bitterness above 26C day temp; Botrytis (gray mold) and downy mildew (Bremia lactucae) in the dense frilly canopy where moisture is trapped; aphids and thrips sheltering deep in ruffles; bottom/lettuce drop (Sclerotinia/Rhizoctonia) on saturated crowns in DFT/Kratky; red color fade in Lollo Rosso under low light; pythium root rot when water temp exceeds 24C; Ca-Mg interveinal yellowing; nitrate accumulation under low light short days
harvest_indicator: Dense frilly loose-leaf rosette 18-22cm wide, 150-220g whole-head; Lollo Rosso shows deep red-bronze tips with green base, Lollo Bionda bright lime-green; leaves crisp and turgid, ruffles fully expanded but heart still tight before any seed-stalk elongation; cut day 30-45
source: Cornell CEA lettuce handbook (tipburn DLI), Rijk Zwaan Lollo datasheets, Johnny's Selected Seeds, Howard Resh Hydroponic Food Production, e-GRO Alert 4.31, ScienceInHydroponics
tips: Lollo Rosso (red) and Lollo Bionda (green) are frilly loose-leaf types prized for texture and color in salad mixes — they fetch a premium over plain green leaf, so target clean, blemish-free frills.

Sow 1 pelleted seed per rockwool/phenolic plug, germinate at 18-20C in darkness 2-3 days, then move under 14-16h light; lettuce seed goes thermo-dormant above 28C so chill-imbibe seed at 10-15C overnight if nursery is hot.

Run EC 0.6 at seedling, 1.0-1.2 vegetative, and hold mature EC at 1.2-1.4 (never above 1.6) — slower growth lets calcium keep pace with the canopy and is the single biggest lever against inner-leaf tip burn.
Keep pH 5.6-6.0 (full range 5.5-6.2); above 6.5 iron and manganese lock out and the frilly heart pales.
Space 18-22cm head-to-head (roughly 20-25 plants/m2 in NFT/DFT) so the broad ruffled rosettes never touch — touching leaves trap moisture and invite Botrytis.

Cap PPFD around 200-250 umol/m2/s and DLI near 14-16 mol/m2/day; Cornell CEA finds tip burn spikes above 17 DLI with vertical fans and above 12 without, so add a vertical airflow fan over the canopy before pushing light higher.
Hold canopy humidity 55-65% by day with continuous gentle airflow blowing across the rosette tops to drive transpiration and pull calcium into the new inner leaves.

Lower the K:Ca ratio toward ~1.25:1 and dose Cal-Mag so solution calcium sits near 150-200 ppm; if tip burn still appears, foliar-spray calcium chloride at 400-800 ppm Ca twice weekly onto the inner leaves.

In Philippine lowland heat, chill the reservoir to 18-22C (max 24C) — warm nutrient drives bolting, dissolves out oxygen, and invites pythium; keep dissolved oxygen above 5-6 mg/L with an air stone in DFT/Kratky.
Use 30-40% shade cloth in the dry season and aim day temp 18-24C, night 12-18C; sustained day temps above 26C trigger bolting, milky latex, and bitter leaves within days.

Inspect frill undersides and the rosette interior weekly with a hand lens for aphids and thrips — release ladybird beetles or Aphidius, and spot-treat with neem oil or insecticidal soap at first sign rather than blanket spraying.
Lollo Rosso reddens best with strong light and cooler nights plus a touch of phosphorus; if color washes out, raise PPFD and let night temp drop to 14-16C in the last 10 days before harvest.
Stagger sowings every 7-10 days for a continuous cut and pull whole heads by day 45 in hot months before bolting — over-mature Lollo turns coarse and bitter and crushes easily in the bag.
Harvest at first light when leaves are fully turgid, handle by the base only, and pre-cool to 2-4C within an hour; the airy frills bruise fast and wilt if packed warm or crammed tight.

risks.title: Inner-leaf tip burn
risks.description: Margins of the young inner leaves brown and die from localized calcium deficiency. The frilly heart encloses the growing point, blocks airflow, and keeps transpiration low so calcium never reaches the new leaves even when the solution has plenty.
risks.mitigation: Hold EC 1.2-1.4 (max 1.6), keep day humidity 55-65%, and run a vertical airflow fan directly over the canopy. Lower K:Ca toward 1.25:1, keep solution Ca ~150-200 ppm, cap DLI near 14-16, and foliar-spray CaCl2 at 400-800 ppm Ca twice weekly if symptoms start.

risks.title: Botrytis gray mold in the canopy
risks.description: The dense ruffled rosette traps free moisture; Botrytis cinerea infects when leaves stay wet 8-12h with canopy humidity above 70-90%, rotting interior and lower leaves often unseen until collapse.
risks.mitigation: Space heads 18-22cm so leaves never touch, keep canopy RH below 70%, vent or run fans to dry foliage before nightfall, avoid overhead wetting, and promptly strip any dead or yellowing lower leaves that shelter spores.

risks.title: Bolting and bitterness
risks.description: Day temperatures above 26C in the Philippine lowlands push the plant to elongate a seed stalk; leaves develop milky latex, turn bitter, and lose market value.
risks.mitigation: Use 30-40% shade cloth in the dry season, chill the reservoir to 18-22C, keep day temp 18-24C and night 12-18C, limit photoperiod to 12-14h, and harvest by day 40-45 in hot months before the heart lifts.

risks.title: Downy mildew (Bremia lactucae)
risks.description: Cool, humid spells with long leaf-wetness periods let Bremia produce angular yellow blotches with white downy growth on leaf undersides; spores need free water to germinate and spread fast through a crowded crop.
risks.mitigation: Keep foliage dry, ventilate to break leaf wetness, hold canopy RH below 70%, remove infected and shaded lower leaves, choose downy-mildew-resistant Lollo lines where available, and improve plant spacing for air penetration.

risks.title: Root rot / pythium from warm water
risks.description: When reservoir temperature climbs above 24C, dissolved oxygen drops and Pythium attacks the roots, causing brown slimy roots, wilting, and bottom rot of the crown in DFT/Kratky.
risks.mitigation: Chill or insulate the reservoir to 18-22C, keep dissolved oxygen above 5-6 mg/L with air stones, shade the tank, sanitize channels between crops, and avoid letting crowns sit in stagnant saturated media.

risks.title: Aphid and thrips infestation in the frills
risks.description: The deep ruffles give aphids and thrips a hidden refuge from the canopy view; colonies build undetected, distort new leaves, and contaminate the heart of premium salad heads.
risks.mitigation: Scout frill undersides and the rosette interior weekly with a hand lens, install screens and yellow/blue sticky cards, release ladybird beetles or Aphidius wasps, and spot-treat with neem oil or insecticidal soap at first detection.

growth_stages.description: Sow one pelleted Lollo seed per rockwool/phenolic plug, barely covered, in a humid nursery tray.
actions: Hold 18-20C in darkness; chill-imbibe seed overnight at 10-15C if nursery is hot to avoid thermo-dormancy

actions: Provide 14-16h light at low PPFD once cotyledons show
Begin a very dilute feed at EC 0.4-0.6, pH 5.6

growth_stages.description: First true frilly leaves form in the nursery channel; roots reach through the plug.
Move sturdy seedlings with 3-4 true leaves into the main NFT/DFT/Kratky channel.
actions: Ensure water temp 18-22C and DO above 5-6 mg/L

growth_stages.description: Frilly rosette expands rapidly; canopy fills and color develops in Lollo Rosso.
actions: Keep day temp below 24C, DLI 14-16, humidity 55-65%
Run vertical airflow over the canopy and scout undersides for aphids

growth_stages.description: Heads near full size; red tips deepen on Lollo Rosso, ruffles fully expand, heart stays tight.
actions: Hold EC at 1.4 (do not exceed 1.6) to limit tip burn
Boost PPFD slightly and let night temp drop to 14-16C to intensify red color
Keep canopy RH below 70% and foliage dry to block Botrytis and downy mildew

growth_stages.description: Cut whole heads at 18-22cm wide, 150-220g, before any bolting or bitterness.
actions: Cut at the crown with a sanitized blade at first light when leaves are turgid
Handle by the base only and pack loose to protect the airy frills
Pre-cool to 2-4C within an hour for shelf life

common_issues: Powdery mildew; vine breakage; poor netting from EC swings
harvest_indicator: Full slip from vine, sweet aroma, raised tan netting on rind
source: Rijk Zwaan melon catalog, Dealzer, Agri Farming, NatureTech
tips: Cantaloupe distinct from generic melon — netted rind, orange flesh, slip-free harvest signal.
Maintain even fertigation during fruit sizing or netting will be patchy.
Bump EC to 2.8 last 2 weeks for sugar (Brix) build-up.
Hammock or sling each fruit at golf-ball size — vines snap under weight.

risks.title: Powdery mildew
risks.description: White dust on large leaves during humid spells.
risks.mitigation: Airflow; prune lower leaves; potassium bicarbonate or sulfur spray weekly.

risks.title: Hollow heart / poor pollination
risks.description: Indoor plants get few bees, fruit aborts or hollow-centered.
risks.mitigation: Hand-pollinate male to female flowers AM with brush; keep humidity 55-70%.

risks.title: Vine breakage
risks.description: Heavy fruit (1-2kg) snaps unsupported vines.
risks.mitigation: Mesh slings clipped to overhead trellis line as soon as fruit forms.
