actions: Prune lateral suckers below fruit zone

growth_stages.description: Yellow male and female flowers appear.
Full slip stage — fruit detaches with light pull.
actions: Cure 2-3 days at room temp before refrigerating

common_issues: Tip burn (inner-leaf Ca deficiency) at high EC, low airflow, or rapid growth; bolting and bitterness above 26C day temp; aphids and thrips hidden deep in the dense rosette; bottom/sclerotinia rot at saturated, warm crown; pythium/root rot when water temp exceeds 24C and DO drops; downy mildew (Bremia) in cool humid spells; iron-deficiency interveinal chlorosis if pH drifts above 6.5; soft, loose heads under low light (DLI under 12); slug and snail damage in open lowland setups
harvest_indicator: Dense uniform rosette 18-25cm wide, single core; one basal cut releases 40-60 baby-leaf-sized leaves; firm springy crown, no seed-stalk elongation; whole-head weight 150-280g at day 28-45 in tropical conditions
source: Rijk Zwaan Salanova datasheets (Euler/Barlach/Xem RZ), Johnny's Salanova catalog, PLOS One 2019 Salanova NFT trial, Cornell CEA Lettuce Handbook, Alabama Coop Ext, e-gro tipburn alert
tips: Salanova/Multileaf is bred so one cut at the core releases 40-60 uniform baby-leaf-sized leaves, yielding 40%+ more ready-to-pack mix per area than hand-picked baby leaf, with roughly double the shelf life.

Match the type to your market: Euler RZ (green butter) and Barlach RZ (red butter) for soft sweet mixes, Xem RZ (red oak) for color and incised/sweet-crisp types for crunch; all green and red colors fit one NFT/DFT workflow.
Spacing: transplant at high density then run on 18-20cm head-to-head centers (about 25-30 plants/m2) in NFT/DFT so each rosette fills out round and uniform without crowding the core.
Ramp EC in steps: 0.6 mS/cm in the nursery, 1.0-1.2 at transplant, holding 1.5-1.6 to harvest; keep EC under 1.8 in hot months because high salts block calcium uptake and trigger tip burn.
Hold pH 5.6-6.2 (target 5.8) for balanced micronutrient uptake; let it drift above 6.5 and you get interveinal iron chlorosis on the youngest leaves.

Tip burn is an internal calcium-transport problem, not a root-zone shortfall: run a paddle/vertical fan blowing air down onto the canopy (Cornell target ~140 cfm per m2 of pond) to drive transpiration and move Ca into the fast-growing inner leaves.
Aim for a Daily Light Integral of 14-17 mol/m2/day; below 12 DLI heads stay loose and soft, but you cannot push high light without matching downward airflow or tip burn worsens.
Keep root-zone/water temperature 18-22C; in PH lowland heat use a chiller or shaded reservoir, as above 24C dissolved oxygen falls and pythium root rot sets in fast.
Use the gold-standard Cornell-style leafy recipe (N ~150-200, Ca ~90-150, Mg 50 ppm); supplement Cal-Mag at 0.5g/L weekly during peak vegetative growth in fast tropical cycles.

In the lowland tropics run 30-50% shade cloth in the dry-season midday and prefer heat-tolerant Salanova selections; day temps above 26C cause bolting, latex bitterness, and loss of the dense one-cut form.
Maintain dissolved oxygen above 5-6 mg/L (target 7-8) with airstones or good NFT film flow; white firm roots indicate healthy oxygenation, brown slimy roots signal pythium.

Scout the rosette base weekly with a torch: aphids and thrips hide deep between overlapping leaves; deploy yellow sticky traps and spot-treat with insecticidal soap or neem at first sign rather than a blanket spray.

Harvest in the cool early morning when leaves are fully turgid; one clean cut at the crown with a sanitized blade, then cool to 2-4C within an hour to lock in the premium shelf life Salanova is sold on.

Salanova commands a premium with restaurants and salad packers, so grade for uniform head size and intact leaf shape; in PH metros expect roughly PHP 350-750/kg for clean hydroponic ready-to-eat product.

risks.title: Tip burn (inner-leaf calcium deficiency)
risks.description: Margins of the young inner leaves brown and die back during rapid growth or under high light, even when calcium is adequate in the nutrient solution, because transpiration is too low to carry Ca into the enclosed core leaves.
risks.mitigation: Run constant downward airflow over the canopy (paddle/vertical fan ~140 cfm/m2), hold EC under 1.8 and humidity 55-70%, keep DLI matched to airflow (do not exceed 12 DLI without strong fans), and choose tip-burn-tolerant Salanova selections.

risks.title: Bolting and bitterness in tropical heat
risks.description: Day temperatures above 26C trigger premature seed-stalk elongation; the rosette loosens, leaves turn bitter with milky latex, and the dense one-cut structure is lost, making heads unmarketable.
risks.mitigation: Use 30-50% shade cloth at midday in the dry season, keep water temp 18-22C with a chiller, cap growlights at 14h, prefer heat-tolerant varieties, and harvest by day 35-40 in hot months.

risks.title: Pythium and root rot
risks.description: Warm water above 24C with low dissolved oxygen lets Pythium and other water molds colonize roots, turning them brown and slimy; plants wilt at midday and stunt despite adequate nutrients.
risks.mitigation: Hold water temp 18-22C (chiller or shaded reservoir), keep DO above 5-6 mg/L with airstones or strong NFT film flow, sanitize channels between crops, and avoid stagnant warm pockets in the reservoir.

risks.title: Aphids and thrips in the dense rosette
risks.description: The tightly overlapping multileaf head shelters aphid colonies and thrips deep at the core where sprays cannot reach, contaminating the ready-to-eat product and spreading viruses.
risks.mitigation: Inspect the rosette base weekly with a torch, hang yellow/blue sticky traps, release ladybugs or lacewings in enclosed setups, and spot-treat early with insecticidal soap or neem at label rate before colonies establish.

risks.title: Downy mildew (Bremia lactucae)
risks.description: Cool, humid, poorly ventilated conditions produce angular yellow patches on upper leaves with white downy sporulation underneath, rapidly rotting leaves and rendering heads unsaleable.
risks.mitigation: Keep humidity 55-70% with active air movement, avoid wetting foliage, space heads at 18-20cm for canopy drying, remove infected plants promptly, and select Bremia-resistant Salanova lines where available.

risks.title: Bottom rot and crown collapse
risks.description: A saturated, warm crown sitting in stagnant film invites Sclerotinia/Rhizoctonia bottom rot; lower leaves brown and slime, and the head collapses from the base near harvest.
risks.mitigation: Keep the crown above the nutrient film, ensure free drainage and airflow under the canopy, hold water temp under 22C, harvest promptly once mature, and sanitize channels and net cups between cycles.

actions: Set water temp 18-22C, DO above 5 mg/L

Reduce EC slightly to limit nitrate before harvest

growth_stages.description: Single basal cut at the crown releases 40-60 uniform baby leaves as ready-to-eat mix.

Cool to 2-4C within one hour for premium shelf life
Grade for uniform head size and pack as ready-to-eat mix

common_issues: Pods turn fibrous if over-mature; aphids; fruit borer
harvest_indicator: Pods 6-9cm long, snap easily, smooth glossy skin
source: DA-BPI, GrowByCity Philippines, Hydroforia, Oklahoma State Extension
tips: Smooth-pod cultivars (e.g. Smooth Green, Clemson Smooth) lack spines on pods and stems — easier handling, less skin irritation than spiny types.
Harvest every 1-2 days at 6-9cm — pods turn woody fast above 10cm.
Soak seeds 12-24h before sowing to break hard seed coat.
Self-pollinating; no hand-pollination needed but bees boost set indoors.

risks.title: Fibrous overripe pods
risks.description: Pods harden into inedible fiber if missed even by 1-2 days.
risks.mitigation: Daily harvest checks; remove oversized pods to keep plant productive.

risks.title: Aphids and whitefly
risks.description: Sap-suckers on tender new growth and pod undersides.
risks.mitigation: Yellow sticky traps; spray insecticidal soap weekly; release ladybugs.

risks.title: Fruit borer (Earias)
risks.description: Larvae tunnel into pods leaving exit holes.
risks.mitigation: Insect mesh exclusion; pheromone traps; remove infested pods immediately.

growth_stages.description: Soak seeds 12-24h then sow in warm rockwool or coco plug.
Move to Dutch Bucket once 4-5 true leaves.

actions: Wear gloves even with smooth varieties — leaves still mildly irritating

common_issues: BER; cat-facing on cool nights; russet mites; fruit cracking; greenback shoulder
harvest_indicator: Large globe fruit 150-300g, fully colored shoulder-to-blossom-end, slight give when pressed
source: Rijk Zwaan Climberley/Beorange datasheets, UF/IFAS hydroponic tomato guide, Kai farm calibration
tips: Slicer/beefsteak varieties (e.g. Climberley, Beorange, Big Beef, Trust) need strong trellising — fruit reaches 200-400g.
Prune to single leader and remove all suckers weekly; lower yield count but bigger fruit.
Drop nighttime temperature 5-7C below day temp to trigger flowering and improve fruit set.
Hand-pollinate by tapping flower trusses mid-morning when humidity is 60-70%.
Reduce EC by 0.3-0.5 during ripening to push sugar concentration and flavor.
Top the plant once it reaches trellis height — let last 2 trusses ripen, then terminate.
Remove lower leaves below the first ripening truss to improve airflow and reduce fungal pressure.

risks.title: Blossom-end rot (BER)
risks.description: Sunken black lesion at fruit base from localized calcium deficiency — large slicer fruit is highly prone.
risks.mitigation: Hold EC steady (avoid >0.4 swings), supplement Cal-Mag weekly, keep humidity 65-75%, ensure root zone never dries between irrigations.

risks.title: Fruit cracking
risks.description: Concentric or radial splits on shoulder from sudden rehydration after stress or rapid sugar accumulation.
risks.mitigation: Maintain consistent fertigation cycles (4-6 short pulses/day), avoid drought-flood pattern, harvest at breaker stage in rainy season.

risks.title: Cat-facing / zippering
risks.description: Deformed scarred fruit from cold nights (<13C) during flower formation.
risks.mitigation: Keep night temp >=16C, avoid early-season transplants, remove malformed first trusses to redirect energy.

risks.title: Russet mites
risks.description: Microscopic mites bronze the lower stems and leaves; severe infestations collapse the plant.
risks.mitigation: Scout weekly with 10x loupe at stem base, apply sulfur spray at first sign, isolate infected plants immediately.

risks.title: Botrytis (gray mold)
risks.description: Fuzzy gray sporulation on stems, leaves, fruit — favored by humidity >85% and stagnant air.
risks.mitigation: Run circulation fans 24/7, prune lower leaves, vent during cool wet nights, avoid wetting foliage.

growth_stages.description: Sow 1-2 seeds 5mm deep in pre-soaked rockwool plug.

actions: Move to bright light immediately upon emergence

growth_stages.description: True leaves develop, root system establishes.
actions: Pot up to 4-inch block at 6 leaves
Pinch to single seedling per plug

growth_stages.description: Move to Dutch Bucket with perlite/coco mix when first flower truss visible.
actions: Bury stem deep to encourage adventitious roots
Install trellis line and clip stem

growth_stages.description: Rapid vine extension and leaf production.
actions: Train to single leader on twine

growth_stages.description: Trusses with 4-7 yellow flowers form every 3 leaves.
actions: Tap trusses mid-morning to release pollen

growth_stages.description: Green fruit swells; thin to 4-5 fruit per truss for slicer size.
actions: Maintain Ca/K balance, monitor for BER on first trusses

growth_stages.description: Fruit turns from mature green through breaker to full color.
actions: Drop EC to 3.0 to concentrate sugars
Lower-leaf prune up to first ripening truss

growth_stages.description: Continuous pick every 2-3 days as trusses ripen sequentially.
actions: Twist fruit at calyx joint or use pruners
Pick at breaker for shipping, vine-ripe for direct sale
Top plant once trellis reached and let final trusses finish

common_issues: Tip burn (localized Ca deficiency) under fast growth/high EC/low airflow; bolting and bitterness above ~26-28C day temp; Pythium/bottom rot in warm saturated root zone; downy mildew (Bremia) in cool wet humid spells; lettuce drop (Sclerotinia/Rhizoctonia) at crown; aphids (Nasonovia, Myzus) sheltering in the open heart; thrips and leafminer in PH lowland screenhouses; ammonium toxicity and pH drift; root browning from low dissolved oxygen / water above 24C
harvest_indicator: Full rounded slightly flat-bottomed Batavia head, crisp wavy mid-green outer leaves with a blanched dense heart, firm and heavy in hand, 22-28cm diameter, 250-350g whole-head weight at 30-45 days; harvest before any seed-stalk/bolting begins
source: Rijk Zwaan Olmetie RZ datasheet (rijkzwaan.in); Cornell CEA Lettuce Handbook; UF/IFAS HS1422; ProduceGrower leafy-greens guide; Phil hydroponic refs; Seed World (Bl:37EU)
tips: Olmetie RZ is a green Batavia (summer-crisp) type bred by Rijk Zwaan for indoor/hydroponic production, not a romaine; it makes a round, open, crisp head with supple wavy leaves that pack and handle easily.
As a Batavia it is naturally heat-tolerant and slow to bolt, making it one of the better lettuce choices for Philippine lowland CEA where many butterhead/romaine types fail.
Hold EC 0.8-1.0 from transplant in hot months and 1.2-1.6 in cooler/dry-season runs; high EC drives faster but tip-burn-prone growth, so back off when day temps climb.
Keep pH 5.6-6.2 (drifts up as roots take nitrate); dose acid in small steps and re-check daily because a small reservoir swings fast in tropical heat.
Target a transplant spacing of about 18-22cm head-to-head (roughly 20-25 plants/m2 final) in NFT/DFT so the open Batavia head can expand without trapping moisture.
Aim for a vegetative DLI of ~14-17 mol/m2/day (PPFD ~200-250 umol/m2/s for 14-16h) for dense heads; seedlings do well at ~50-200 umol/m2/s.
Cool the nutrient solution to 18-22C with a chiller in PH lowland heat; root zones above 24-25C cut dissolved oxygen and invite Pythium and premature bolting.
Maintain dissolved oxygen near 8 mg/L (airstones or NFT film turbulence) to keep white healthy roots and prevent brown-root collapse.
Tip burn is a calcium-transport disorder, not a true shortage: run gentle constant horizontal airflow over the canopy and keep humidity 55-70% so the inner heart leaves transpire and pull Ca.
Supplement Cal-Mag (~0.5g/L weekly) and never let pH stray outside 5.6-6.2, since pH and EC out of range block Ca uptake even when Ca is present.
Olmetie's resistance package (HR to a wide Bremia/downy-mildew race spread up to Bl:37EU, plus IR/HR to the Nasonovia Nr:0 lettuce aphid) lets you cut routine sprays and lean on IPM instead.
Despite aphid resistance, the open heart can shelter Myzus and thrips: scout the heart weekly and release Aphidius/lacewings or spot-treat with insecticidal soap or neem at first sign.
Sanitize channels, reservoir and plug trays with ~1-3% H2O2 between crops and use clean RO/dechlorinated water to keep Pythium and Sclerotinia (lettuce drop) out of the system.
Harvest in the early morning when leaves are fully turgid and cool the head to ~4C within an hour of cutting to lock in crispness and shelf life.

risks.title: Tip burn
risks.description: Inner heart-leaf margins brown and die back from localized calcium deficiency during rapid growth, worsened by high EC, low airflow, and high humidity that stop the young leaves from transpiring.
risks.mitigation: Hold EC 0.8-1.2 in heat, keep RH 55-70%, run constant gentle horizontal airflow over the canopy, supplement Cal-Mag ~0.5g/L weekly, and keep pH 5.6-6.2 so Ca stays available.

risks.title: Bolting and bitterness in tropical heat
risks.description: Sustained day temps above ~26-28C, common in PH lowlands, push the plant toward a seed stalk; leaves turn bitter and milky and the head loses density even in this heat-tolerant Batavia.
risks.mitigation: Use 30-40% shade cloth in the dry season, chill the solution to 18-22C, limit growlights to 12-14h in hot spells, and harvest by day 35-40 rather than holding heads in the system.

risks.title: Pythium / bottom rot at warm root zone
risks.description: Water above 24C and low dissolved oxygen let Pythium and bottom rot collapse the crown and brown the roots, the single biggest CEA lettuce loss in PH lowland summers.
risks.mitigation: Chill solution to 18-22C, keep DO near 8 mg/L with airstones or NFT turbulence, avoid waterlogged plugs, and sanitize channels with 1-3% H2O2 between crops.

risks.title: Downy mildew (Bremia lactucae)
risks.description: Cool, wet, humid spells and overhead wetting trigger pale angular leaf spots with white sporulation underneath; Olmetie carries broad HR but new races can break resistance over time.
risks.mitigation: Lean on the variety's Bl resistance (up to Bl:37EU), keep foliage dry, ventilate to drop canopy humidity below 85%, space heads for airflow, and rotate any protectant sprays per IPM.

risks.title: Aphids and thrips in the open heart
risks.description: The open Batavia heart shelters Myzus persicae, thrips, and leafminer despite Nr:0 resistance to the currant-lettuce aphid Nasonovia; colonies foul leaves and can vector virus.
risks.mitigation: Scout the heart weekly, deploy yellow/blue sticky traps and exclusion netting, release Aphidius wasps or lacewings preventively, and spot-treat with insecticidal soap or neem at first sighting.

risks.title: pH / EC drift in a small tropical reservoir
risks.description: In PH heat a small reservoir warms and evaporates fast, so EC concentrates and pH climbs as roots strip nitrate, pushing the crop into tip burn or nutrient lockout within a day.
risks.mitigation: Check EC and pH daily, top up with plain pH-balanced water before re-dosing nutrients, shade and insulate the reservoir, and fully refresh solution every 7-10 days.

growth_stages.description: Sow 1 pelleted seed per pre-soaked rockwool or phenolic plug about 3mm deep.
actions: Cover lightly with vermiculite to keep the pellet moist
Hold 18-20C in the dark with no added nutrients

growth_stages.description: Cotyledons emerge within 3-4 days; high lettuce germination heat-sensitivity means keep it cool.
actions: Move to bright light (50-100 umol/m2/s) the moment shoots emerge
Keep 18-22C and RH ~70%, media just moist

growth_stages.description: First 2-3 true leaves form and the root system anchors into the plug.
actions: Start nutrients at EC 0.6 once 2 true leaves show
Thin to a single seedling per plug and keep water 18-22C

growth_stages.description: Move to the production NFT/DFT channel at 4-5 true leaves with a firm root mat.
actions: Confirm DO ~8 mg/L and solution 18-22C before loading

growth_stages.description: Open Batavia frame builds fast; wavy outer leaves expand and the rosette widens.
actions: Run EC 1.2-1.6 (lower end in heat), pH 5.6-6.2
Hold day temp <24C, night <18C; shade 30-40% in dry season
Top up reservoir daily and start canopy airflow to pre-empt tip burn

growth_stages.description: Heart leaves cup inward and blanch, firming the rounded crisp Batavia head.
actions: Drop EC to ~1.2-1.3 the final week to lift flavor and ease tip burn
Increase horizontal airflow over the heart to keep it dry
Scout the heart for tip burn, aphids, thrips and downy mildew

growth_stages.description: Single cut at the base when the head is full, firm, 22-28cm wide and 250-350g, before any bolting.
actions: Cut at the crown with a sanitized blade in the early morning
Cool to ~4C within 1 hour of cutting for maximum shelf life

common_issues: Bean fly (Ophiomyia phaseoli) larvae bore the stem base of seedlings days 3-10; aphids and leafhoppers vector bean mosaic virus; pod borers and cutworms damage pods; spider mites in hot dry air; Fusarium wilt and root/stem rot in over-saturated, warm root zone; bean rust and powdery mildew on dense unventilated canopy; leafminer; flower/pod drop from heat >35C or N excess (lush vine, few pods); blossom-end and pod tip necrosis from Ca/B shortfall; weak nodulation means rhizobia cannot supply N in sterile solution so chlorosis appears if mineral N is cut too far.
harvest_indicator: Pods firm, smooth and bright green, 35-50cm long and ~6-9mm thick, snapping crisply with seeds still small and not bulging; pick 7-12 days after flower opening before pods turn pale, fibrous or lumpy. Harvest every 2-3 days to keep the vine setting new pods.
source: ATI/DA-BPI PH Pole Sitaw guides, Agriculture Monthly PH, PMC9920343 hydroponic bean reduced-N+rhizobia study, HydroExperts & IGWorks hydroponic bean EC/pH datasheets, ECHOcommunity yardlong bean
tips: Use a heavy-feed soilless system: Dutch bucket with perlite/coco or a deep ebb-and-flow bed suits this large, water-hungry climbing vine better than thin NFT channels.
Direct-sow 1-2 seeds per net pot or plug 2-3cm deep; germination is 4-7 days at 25-30C root zone. No cold stratification needed.
Run an EC ramp tuned for a legume: 0.8 seedling, 1.4-1.6 vegetative, ~2.0 at flowering, holding 2.0-2.4 during peak podding; keep below ~2.5 to avoid pod tip burn.
Hold pH 6.0-6.5 (mildly higher than lettuce) to keep iron, Ca and B available for heavy pod set; correct daily as the vine drinks fast in PH heat.

Legume N nuance: rhizobia nodulation is unreliable in a clean hydroponic solution (bacteria are largely inactive/absent), so the plant CANNOT self-supply nitrogen here. Keep mineral N in the feed; cutting N too far causes pale leaves and yield loss (reduced-N hydroponic beans lost 11-35% yield even with inoculation).
But do not overdo N either: excess nitrogen on a legume drives a jungle of vine with few pods. Use a moderate, P- and K-fed bloom formula once flowering starts.
Trellis is mandatory: give each plant a strong vertical string, mesh or bamboo to a 2-3m overhead support; install before the vine starts running (~day 12-15) or growth tangles.
Train tendrils onto the support and top (pinch) the leader near the top wire to push lateral pod-bearing shoots and keep picking within reach.
Space 25-35cm between plants along the trellis (about 8-12 plants/m2 of growing strip) for airflow; crowding feeds rust and powdery mildew.
Self-pollinating: flowers are cleistogamous (selfing inside the keel), so NO hand pollination is needed indoors or in screened houses, unlike ampalaya, upo or patola.
Keep root-zone/water temperature 22-28C and dissolved oxygen high (>5-6 mg/L); warm stagnant solution invites Fusarium and root rot in this crop.

Scout bean fly in the first 10 days (wilting/swollen seedling stems) and aphids/leafhoppers throughout; manage with yellow sticky traps, neem at ~5 mL/L, or spinosad/insecticidal soap, and rogue any mosaic-virus plants.
Harvest pods young at 35-50cm every 2-3 days; continuous picking over 4-8+ weeks is the key driver of total yield, leaving pods to mature shuts down flowering.
Provide 14-16h of strong light (target ~400-600 PPFD at canopy) for indoor runs; this heat-loving lowland crop thrives at 25-33C day and tolerates PH humidity well.

risks.title: Misapplied legume nitrogen (too little or too much)
risks.description: Growers assume sitaw fixes its own N like in soil, but in sterile hydroponic solution rhizobia are inactive so no biological N is added; starving N yields pale, stunted vines and poor pods. Conversely, dumping N produces a lush leafy vine with almost no pod set.
risks.mitigation: Keep a balanced mineral N feed throughout (do not zero it out); at flowering switch to a moderate-N, higher-P/K bloom mix and hold EC ~2.0-2.4. Watch leaf color: uniform mid-green is the target.

risks.title: Trellis failure / vine tangle
risks.description: Sitaw is a vigorous 2-3m+ climber; without timely strong support the canopy collapses, mats together, shades pods, and traps humidity that breeds disease.
risks.mitigation: Install a 2-3m vertical string/mesh/bamboo trellis before day 12-15, anchor to an overhead wire rated for the wet vine weight, train shoots weekly and top the leader at the top wire.

risks.title: Bean fly and sucking pests vectoring mosaic virus
risks.description: Bean fly maggots bore seedling stem bases (days 3-10) and can kill young plants; aphids and leafhoppers transmit bean mosaic virus, causing mottled, distorted leaves and crippled yield.
risks.mitigation: Scout daily early on; use yellow sticky traps, neem (~5 mL/L) or spinosad/insecticidal soap, keep a clean screened area, and immediately remove and discard mosaic-symptom plants to stop spread.

risks.title: Fusarium wilt and root/stem rot in warm wet root zone
risks.description: In tropical heat a saturated, low-oxygen, >28C root zone favors Fusarium and Pythium/Rhizoctonia rots, causing sudden wilting, browning stem base and vine collapse.
risks.mitigation: Hold water temp 22-28C, keep DO >5-6 mg/L, do not let media stay waterlogged in Dutch buckets, sanitize tanks between cycles, and avoid wounding stems during training.

risks.title: Bean rust and powdery mildew on dense canopy
risks.description: Crowded, poorly ventilated, humid PH conditions trigger orange rust pustules and white powdery mildew, defoliating the vine and cutting pod production.
risks.mitigation: Space 25-35cm, prune to open the canopy, run airflow fans, keep humidity nearer 60-70%, and apply sulfur or a preventive biofungicide on first sign.

risks.title: Flower and pod drop from heat or imbalance
risks.description: Sustained temps above ~35C, big EC/moisture swings, or Ca/B shortfall cause blossoms and young pods to abort, slashing the continuous harvest.
risks.mitigation: Shade 20-30% in the hottest hours, stabilize EC/pH and irrigation, and supplement Cal-Mag (~0.5 g/L) plus trace boron to support pod set and prevent pod-tip necrosis.

growth_stages.description: Direct-sow seed into net pots or plugs; radicle and first leaves emerge in warm media.
actions: Sow 1-2 seeds 2-3cm deep per plug, pre-moistened to pH 6.0
Hold root zone 25-30C, no or very dilute feed (EC <=0.8)
Keep media damp but not waterlogged, high DO

growth_stages.description: First true trifoliate leaves expand and the seedling begins to vine; bean fly risk is highest now.
actions: Scout stem bases for bean fly daily; set yellow sticky traps
Transplant into Dutch bucket/ebb-and-flow at 25-35cm spacing

growth_stages.description: Vine runs rapidly and tendrils search for support; rapid leaf and stem growth.
actions: Install/clip onto a 2-3m vertical string or mesh trellis before vines tangle
Raise feed to EC 1.4-1.6, keep mineral N supplied (do not rely on rhizobia)
Train shoots up the support weekly, maintain airflow

growth_stages.description: Cleistogamous (self-pollinating) violet/white flowers open in clusters at nodes; no hand pollination needed.
actions: Shift to moderate-N, higher P/K bloom feed, EC ~2.0 at pH 6.0-6.5
Add Cal-Mag ~0.5 g/L plus trace boron for pod set
Avoid N spikes and keep day temp below ~35C to prevent flower drop

growth_stages.description: Long slender pods elongate quickly from set flowers, lengthening to harvest size.
actions: Hold EC 2.0-2.4, pH 6.0-6.5, steady irrigation to avoid pod tip burn/drop
Keep water temp 22-28C and DO high to fend off root rot
Top the leader at the top wire to push lateral pod shoots

growth_stages.description: Pods reach 35-50cm, firm, smooth and bright green, snapping crisply with small seeds; first picking begins.
actions: Harvest pods 7-12 days after flowering, before seeds bulge or pods turn fibrous
Pick in cool morning hours; cut/snap cleanly without tearing the vine
Continue feeding EC 2.0-2.4 to fuel ongoing pod set

growth_stages.description: Vine keeps flowering and setting new pods for several weeks; frequent picking sustains production.
actions: Pick every 2-3 days; never leave mature pods on the vine (it stops new flowering)
Maintain EC ~2.0-2.4 and replenish Cal-Mag/trace; scout rust, mildew, aphids
Remove spent or diseased foliage to keep canopy open and productive

common_issues: Seed germination erratic and often <50% (propagate by cuttings instead); damping-off (Pythium/Rhizoctonia) on tender cuttings in cool, wet rockwool; Sclerotinia/Septoria leaf spot and stem rot in stagnant humid canopies; root rot (Pythium) when water temp >28C and DO low; loss of leaf sweetness once short-day flowering is triggered (nights >11-12h); aphids, whitefly, spider mites and leaf miners under screen houses; iron and N deficiency cut both biomass and steviol-glycoside (Reb-A) content; chlorosis at neutral-to-alkaline pH (>6.8) from poor micronutrient uptake; tip wilt and salt stress if EC pushed >2.0
harvest_indicator: Bushy upright plant 30-50cm tall, dense dark-green fully-expanded leaves with flower buds just forming; harvest leaves/tips BEFORE flowers open for peak sweetness; leaves snap crisp at maximum thickness; first cut ~55-75 days from rooted cutting, then re-cut every 21-30 days
source: OSU Extension cutting guide; Longdom/ScienceDirect hydroponic nutrient-pH SG study; Romanian J Hort cutting-EC trial; IGWorks hydroponic guide; Purdue/UF germination; GIDC PH grower; Lazada/Shopee PH
tips: Propagate from cuttings, NOT seed: stevia seed germinates erratically (commonly <50%, light-dependent, ~25C), so take 8-10cm tip cuttings with 3-4 nodes from a healthy non-flowering mother plant.
Dip cutting bases in IBA at 1-3 mg/L (or rooting gel), strip lower leaves, and root in pre-soaked rockwool/net pots under mist or a humidity dome; roots appear in ~10-13 days at 24-26C.
Root and raise cuttings at a very low EC of 0.3-0.5 mS/cm; trials show 100% rooting near 0.3 dS/m and best early shoot/leaf growth around 0.7 dS/m before stepping up.
Ramp EC gently: 0.5 seedling/cutting -> 1.0-1.3 vegetative -> 1.4-1.6 mature; hold EC <=1.8 mS/cm because high salts reduce leaf quality and traditional sweet flavor.
Hold pH 5.5-6.5 (aim 5.8-6.2); stevia grows poorly and shows micronutrient chlorosis under neutral-to-alkaline pH (>6.8), so check and correct daily in recirculating systems.
Stevia is a leafy herb feeder: run NFT, DFT, DWC or Kratky and space plants 20-25cm apart (roughly 16-25 plants/m2) so bushy canopies get airflow and even light.
Give 14-16h of light at 250-450 umol/m2/s PPFD for fast vegetative bulking; the long photoperiod also keeps the plant vegetative and delays short-day flowering.
CRITICAL for sweetness: stevia is a short-day plant that flowers when nights exceed ~11-12h; flowering shifts energy from leaves and drops steviol-glycoside content, so harvest leaves BEFORE bloom.
Pinch/top the growing tips above the 4th-6th node early and after each cut to force branching; bushier plants delay flowering, carry more leaf nodes and lift total yield.
Use cut-and-come-again harvesting: cut the top 2/3 (leaving 10-15cm and several lower nodes) and the plant reflushes; expect a fresh cut every 21-30 days for multiple flushes per perennial plant.
Keep root-zone/water temp 20-26C and DO around 5-7 mg/L; above ~28C in DWC, root rot (Pythium) risk rises sharply in tropical PH lowland conditions.
Protect leaf quality: maintain canopy humidity 55-75% with gentle constant airflow to prevent Septoria/Sclerotinia leaf spot and stem rot in dense plantings.
Watch iron and nitrogen: N or Fe deficiency reduces both biomass and the prized rebaudioside-A fraction, so keep a complete leafy-herb micronutrient mix and chelated Fe in solution.
Dry harvested leaves quickly at low heat (35-45C) or air-dry in shade; dried leaves are noticeably sweeter than fresh and store far longer for tea/sweetener use.

risks.title: Flowering ruins leaf sweetness
risks.description: Stevia is a short-day plant: when nights lengthen past ~11-12h it sets flower buds, diverting energy from leaves and sharply lowering steviol-glycoside (stevioside/Reb-A) content, turning leaves bitter.
risks.mitigation: Run a long photoperiod of 14-16h growlight to keep plants vegetative, pinch tips repeatedly to delay bloom, and harvest leaves at first sign of bud BEFORE flowers open.

risks.title: Erratic seed germination
risks.description: Stevia seed is light-dependent and notoriously poor/uneven (often <50%, much lower with poorly filled seed), giving slow, weak, non-uniform stands and wasted nursery space.
risks.mitigation: Skip seed for production; propagate vegetatively from 8-10cm, 3-4 node tip cuttings of a vigorous non-flowering mother plant, IBA 1-3 mg/L, rooted under mist at 24-26C.

risks.title: Damping-off / cutting rot at propagation
risks.description: Fresh cuttings with no roots are very prone to Pythium/Rhizoctonia damping-off and basal rot in cold, saturated, low-oxygen rockwool.
risks.mitigation: Use sterile media, keep propagation EC 0.3-0.5 and water/air temp 24-26C, mist rather than soak, ensure drainage and airflow, and discard any blackened cuttings immediately.

risks.title: Root rot from warm water
risks.description: In tropical PH lowlands DWC/NFT reservoirs can exceed 28C, dropping dissolved oxygen and inviting Pythium root rot that browns roots and wilts the canopy.
risks.mitigation: Hold water temp 20-26C (shade/insulate tank or chill), keep DO 5-7 mg/L with air stones, and add beneficial microbes; reduce EC during heat spikes.

risks.title: Foliar disease in dense canopies
risks.description: Bushy, frequently-cut stevia plus high humidity favors Septoria leaf spot and Sclerotinia/stem rot, producing leaf lesions and rotting stems that cut marketable leaf.
risks.mitigation: Space 20-25cm, keep humidity 55-75% with constant gentle airflow, remove infected leaves, avoid wetting foliage, and rotate harvest to open the canopy.

risks.title: Nutrient deficiency lowers yield and Reb-A
risks.description: Hydroponic studies show N or P/Fe shortfalls reduce leaf biomass AND the proportion of rebaudioside-A (the preferred sweet glycoside), degrading both quantity and quality.
risks.mitigation: Maintain a complete leafy-herb formula with adequate N and chelated Fe, keep pH 5.8-6.2 for micronutrient availability, and top up/refresh solution on EC and volume.

risks.title: Sucking pests under screen houses
risks.description: Aphids, whitefly, spider mites and leaf miners colonize tender stevia tips in warm PH greenhouses, distorting growth and contaminating leaf destined for sweetener.
risks.mitigation: Scout twice weekly, use yellow sticky traps, release predatory mites, and spot-treat with insecticidal soap or neem (~5 ml/L); avoid harvest-interval residues.

growth_stages.description: Take 8-10cm tip cuttings with 3-4 nodes from a healthy, non-flowering mother plant.
actions: Cut just below a node with a sterile blade
Strip lower leaves, keep 2-3 top leaves
Dip base in IBA 1-3 mg/L or rooting gel
Insert into pre-soaked rockwool at pH 5.5-5.8

growth_stages.description: Cuttings form adventitious roots under mist/dome; roots appear by ~day 10-13.
actions: Hold 24-26C and high humidity (80-90%) under dome/mist
Run very low EC 0.3-0.5 mS/cm, pH 5.5-6.0
Keep media moist not soaked; ensure airflow to prevent damping-off

growth_stages.description: Rooted cuttings harden off and are transplanted into the NFT/DFT/DWC channel.
actions: Transplant when roots reach 3-5cm into net pots

growth_stages.description: Plant builds root mass and side shoots; first pinch sets branching structure.
actions: Pinch growing tip above 4th-6th node to force branching
Step EC to 1.0-1.3 mS/cm, hold pH 5.8-6.2

growth_stages.description: Bushy canopy of thick, dark-green sweet leaves develops; this is the leaf-quality phase.
actions: Maintain long 14-16h days to suppress flowering
Scout weekly for aphids/mites and leaf spot

growth_stages.description: Harvest leaves at peak sweetness just before/at first flower-bud, ~55-75 days from cutting.
actions: Cut top 2/3 of plant, leaving 10-15cm and lower nodes
Strip leaves; dry at 35-45C or shade-dry
Pinch any flower buds spotted to protect sweetness

growth_stages.description: Plant reflushes new shoots from retained lower nodes for the next cut-and-come-again flush.
actions: Pinch new tips again to keep bushy and delay bloom
Expect next harvest ~21-30 days after each cut

growth_stages.description: As a perennial, stevia yields multiple flushes; manage for sustained leaf quality over months.
actions: Refresh nutrient solution and clean reservoir regularly
Keep long photoperiod year-round to block short-day flowering
Rejuvenate by hard cut-back when plants get woody/leggy
Take fresh cuttings periodically to renew stock

common_issues: Melon fruit fly (Bactrocera cucurbitae) stinging young fruit, laying eggs that hatch into maggots causing rot and fruit drop (top PH cucurbit pest); downy mildew (Pseudoperonospora cubensis) angular yellow leaf lesions at 15-30C high humidity; powdery mildew (Podosphaera xanthii) white coating at 20-26C and poor airflow; gummy stem blight and Fusarium wilt; aphids (Aphis gossypii) and whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) vectoring mosaic and Squash leaf curl PH virus (begomovirus); thrips; spider mites in dry heat; poor fruit set from missing daytime pollinators when no hand pollination; excess nitrogen causing rank vine and few female flowers; blossom-end and B/Ca deficiency disorders at high EC; Pythium root rot in warm stagnant solution; Mg interveinal chlorosis under heavy fruit load; bitter over-mature fruit turning yellow/orange and splitting if harvest delayed
harvest_indicator: Harvest immature 12-20 days after pollination while skin is shiny dark green, ridges firm and seeds soft and white inside; PH-type fruit 20-30cm, 200-350g; cut stalk leaving 1-2cm stem; pick every 2-3 days before seeds harden or fruit turns bitter, yellow-orange and splits
source: World Vegetable Center/AVRDC Bitter Gourd Cultural Practices (Pub 17-818), UF/IFAS HS1271, businessdiary.com.ph Ampalaya Hydroponics, gardeningtips.in, East-West Seed types, DA Bantay Presyo
tips: Grow ampalaya in 18-25L Dutch buckets or deep ebb-and-flow with 70% coco coir + 30% perlite media; it is a vigorous heavy feeder, so run one plant per bucket with 50-80cm between plants (roughly 2-3 plants/m2 of bed).

Ramp EC gradually: 1.2 mS/cm at seedling, 1.9 through vegetative climbing, 2.3 as female flowers and first fruit appear, then hold 2.4-2.6 mS/cm at heavy fruiting (about 1700-1850ppm at the 0.7 conversion); keep pH 5.8-6.5.

Build a strong overhead/vertical trellis at least 2-2.5m tall (bamboo, GI pipe, or heavy netting in a balag/A-frame); ampalaya vines climb fast within 2 weeks of transplant and crop continuously for 4-6 months only with sturdy support.

Ampalaya is monoecious with separate male and female flowers and male flowers outnumber females ~25:1, with males opening up to 1-2 weeks BEFORE the first females, so do not panic over early male-only flowering.

Indoors or in insect-screened setups HAND-POLLINATE every morning: pick a freshly opened male flower (no swelling behind the petals) and dab pollen onto the female stigma; do it early (flowers open at sunrise, pollen is dead by midday) since each flower lives only one day.
Identify female flowers by the miniature warty bitter-gourd ovary at the base of the bloom; pollinated females set fruit in 2-5 days, so tag pollinated flowers to track the 12-20 day harvest window.

Feed as a heavy fruiting feeder: shift to a high-potassium bloom formula (N:K near 1:1.5-1:2) once flowering starts and avoid excess nitrogen, which throws rank vine and leaves with few female flowers and poor set.

Supplement Cal-Mag at 0.5-1.0g/L weekly during fruiting to prevent blossom-end/Ca and B disorders and Mg interveinal chlorosis under load; keep root-zone water 22-26C and dissolved oxygen >=5-6 mg/L to avoid Pythium root rot in PH heat.

Provide 12-14h strong light (PPFD ~400-600 umol/m2/s at canopy); prune lateral branches on the lower 10 nodes until the main runner reaches the top of the trellis, then leave 4-6 upper laterals and pinch the main tip to push female flowers and early cropping.

Hang cue-lure/methyl-eugenol pheromone traps plus yellow sticky traps from first flowering for melon fruit fly (Bactrocera cucurbitae), and BAG each young fruit in paper or fine nylon-net sleeves 2-3 days after pollination to block egg-laying.

Manage downy mildew (angular yellow lesions) and powdery mildew (white coating) in humid PH air with 60-75% RH, wide spacing, strong canopy airflow, and rotate protectants (potassium bicarbonate 5g/L, mancozeb, neem 3-5ml/L, copper) at first spotting.

Control aphids and whitefly (begomovirus/mosaic vectors) with neem oil 3% or imidacloprid 0.3ml/L, use reflective mulch to repel them, scout leaf undersides weekly, and rogue out any leaf-curl or mosaic plants immediately.

Harvest immature and tender 12-20 days after pollination while the skin is shiny dark green and seeds are still soft and white; tender ampalaya is less fibrous, and cutting every 2-3 days keeps the vine setting new fruit for continuous harvest.

Check EC daily during fruiting - a fruit-loaded ampalaya drinks heavily and EC creeps up fast in hot weather; flush the system with plain pH-5.8 water for 1-2 days between major nutrient resets and field-sanitize fallen/stung fruit daily.

risks.title: Poor fruit set from no daytime pollinators
risks.description: Ampalaya is monoecious and insect (bee) pollinated by day; indoors or in insect-screened houses no bees reach the flowers, and since each flower opens at sunrise and dies by midday, unpollinated females simply yellow and drop with zero fruit.
risks.mitigation: Hand-pollinate every open female each MORNING - transfer pollen from a fresh male flower onto the female stigma before pollen loses viability at midday; introduce a beehive instead if running a large screened tunnel.

risks.title: Trellis failure and tangled vines
risks.description: Vines elongate rapidly and crop for 4-6 months; a flimsy net or short stake collapses under the canopy, fruit and lateral mass, smothering airflow and causing fruit rot and broken vines.
risks.mitigation: Erect a 2-2.5m bamboo/GI-pipe overhead (balag) or A-frame trellis before vines run, train the main stem up and tie loosely, and prune lower laterals so the canopy stays open and supported.

risks.title: Melon fruit fly (Bactrocera cucurbitae)
risks.description: Adult flies sting young soft fruit and lay eggs; maggots tunnel inside causing yellowing, rotting and premature fruit drop - the single biggest cucurbit yield killer in PH lowlands, with losses up to 80-100% if unmanaged.
risks.mitigation: Deploy cue-lure/methyl-eugenol pheromone and yellow sticky traps from first flowering, bag young fruit in paper/net sleeves 2-3 days after pollination, and collect and destroy stung/fallen fruit daily for sanitation.

risks.title: Downy and powdery mildew
risks.description: Humid PH conditions drive downy mildew (angular yellow lesions, grey sporulation on leaf undersides at 15-30C) and powdery mildew (white powder on shaded lower leaves at 20-26C); both defoliate the vine and shrink fruit yield.
risks.mitigation: Hold RH 60-75% with strong airflow and wide spacing, avoid wetting foliage, avoid excess N, and rotate protectants - potassium bicarbonate 5g/L, mancozeb, neem 3-5ml/L, or copper/sulfur - at the first lesions.

risks.title: Aphid/whitefly-borne virus
risks.description: Aphis gossypii and Bemisia tabaci transmit mosaic and Squash leaf curl Philippines virus (begomovirus) that curl/cup leaves, mottle foliage and deform fruit; infected vines do not recover.
risks.mitigation: Scout undersides weekly, knock down vectors with neem oil 3% or imidacloprid 0.3ml/L, deploy reflective mulch and yellow traps, raise seedlings in insect-proof net, and rogue symptomatic plants at once.

risks.title: Rank vegetative growth and weak set from excess nitrogen
risks.description: Over-feeding nitrogen produces lush vine and dark leaves but delays and suppresses female flowering, so the plant runs to foliage with few fruit - a classic cucurbit fruiting-stage error.
risks.mitigation: Cap fruiting EC at 2.4-2.6 with a high-K bloom formula (N:K ~1:1.5-1:2), prune lower laterals to channel energy to fruiting nodes, and top the main runner once it tops the trellis.

risks.title: Root rot and Ca/Mg disorder under load in PH heat
risks.description: PH heat warms the nutrient solution; above ~28C dissolved oxygen falls and Pythium root rot sets in, while fast fruit growth on a heavy diet outpaces Ca/Mg transport, giving blossom-end disorder and interveinal yellowing.
risks.mitigation: Keep water 22-26C with shade and aeration, hold DO >=5-6 mg/L via air stones or frequent ebb cycles, add Cal-Mag 0.5-1.0g/L weekly, and hold pH 5.8-6.5 for nutrient availability.

growth_stages.description: Nick or soak the hard ampalaya seed, then sow 1 seed per moist coco-coir plug ~15-20mm deep.
actions: Pre-soak seed 24h in lukewarm water (optionally nick the seed coat) to speed germination
Sow into moist (not soggy) coco plug, pH 5.8

growth_stages.description: Cotyledons emerge in roughly 5-8 days at warm temperatures.
actions: Keep 25-32C and high humidity until emergence
Begin dilute feed EC 1.2 once cotyledons open
Give 12h bright light to prevent leggy stretching

growth_stages.description: First 2-4 true leaves develop; seedling readies for transplant at 10-15cm tall.
actions: Transplant to 18-25L bucket when 3-4 true leaves / 10-15cm tall, keeping root ball intact

growth_stages.description: Vine grows fast and is trained up the trellis; lateral framework forms over 2-4 weeks.
actions: Train main stem up a 2-2.5m strong trellis, tie loosely
Prune lateral branches on the lower ~10 nodes; space plants 50-80cm

growth_stages.description: Yellow flowers open; many male flowers come first (~25:1), then females show a tiny warty ovary at the base.
actions: Lift EC to 2.3 and shift to a high-K bloom feed (N:K ~1:1.5-1:2)
Hand-pollinate each open female in the MORNING (pollen dies by midday) from a fresh male flower
Leave 4-6 upper laterals and pinch the main tip to push female flowers
Hang cue-lure fruit-fly traps and yellow sticky traps; add Cal-Mag 0.5-1.0g/L

growth_stages.description: Pollinated ovaries set in 2-5 days and swell into ridged green fruit; load builds across the upper laterals.
actions: Hold fruiting EC 2.4-2.6, pH 5.8-6.5, check EC daily
Bag young fruit in paper/net sleeves 2-3 days after pollination to block fruit fly
Maintain DO >=5-6 mg/L, RH 60-75% and strong airflow against mildew
Keep hand-pollinating new females each morning

growth_stages.description: First tender fruit ready 12-20 days after pollination while skin is shiny dark green and seeds soft.
actions: Harvest when fruit is 20-30cm / 200-350g, shiny green, seeds still soft and white
Cut the stalk with scissors leaving 1-2cm stem
Pick every 2-3 days before fruit turns bitter/yellow to trigger new flowering

growth_stages.description: Vine keeps flowering and fruiting for months; total ~2.5-4kg marketable per plant over the run.
actions: Keep hand-pollinating new females each morning
Hold EC 2.4-2.6 and replenish Cal-Mag weekly under load
Remove over-mature/yellow fruit promptly to sustain set
Stay on mildew and fruit-fly IPM through the whole 4-6 month run

common_issues: Melon fruit fly (Bactrocera cucurbitae) stinging young fruit causing maggots and fruit drop; downy mildew (Pseudoperonospora cubensis) angular yellow leaf lesions in humid PH conditions; powdery mildew white coating on leaves; aphids (Aphis gossypii) and whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) vectoring mosaic/leaf-curl viruses; thrips; red pumpkin beetle; poor fruit set from missing night pollinators (no hand pollination); blossom-end rot and misshapen fruit from Ca/B deficiency at high EC; root-zone hypoxia and Pythium root rot in warm stagnant solution; bitter or yellowing over-mature fruit if harvest delayed; Mg deficiency interveinal chlorosis under heavy fruit load
harvest_indicator: Harvest young/tender 10-18 days after pollination while skin is soft and glossy green (not yellowing), fingernail pierces rind easily, fine hairs still present; club fruit 20-45cm long, 600-1200g each; cut with 2-3cm stem; pick every 2-3 days to keep vine productive
source: smgardener.com hydroponic lauki guide, gardeningtips.in, LaNiTex Hydro gourd guide, JABB 2025 bottle gourd floral biology, StuartXchange Upo (PH), TNAU Agritech, DA Bantay Presyo, Web validation
tips: Run upo in 18-25L Dutch buckets or deep ebb-and-flow with 70% coco coir + 30% perlite media; it is a vigorous heavy feeder, so one plant per bucket only with 80-120cm between plants.

Ramp EC gradually: 1.2 mS/cm at seedling, 1.8 through vegetative climbing, 2.2 at first flowers, then hold 2.2-2.5 mS/cm during heavy fruiting; keep pH 5.8-6.2 and TDS roughly 1100-1300ppm at peak load.
Build a VERY strong overhead trellis or A-frame (bamboo/GI pipe rated for 15-25kg per vine) at least 2-2.5m tall; upo fruits are heavy and a flimsy net will collapse mid-season.
Support each developing fruit with a cloth/net sling or soft cradle tied to the frame so the fruit weight does not snap the peduncle or tear the vine off the trellis.

Upo is monoecious and night/evening-flowering with white moth-pollinated flowers, so indoors/in screened setups HAND-POLLINATE: pick a freshly opened male flower in late afternoon (anthesis is 5-8pm) and dust pollen onto the receptive female stigma the same evening or by early next morning.

Identify female flowers by the miniature bottle-shaped ovary at the base; pollinate every open female (stigma stays receptive ~6h before to ~30h after opening) to push fruit set toward the 80% seen with hand pollination.

Heavy feeder: target an N:K ratio near 1:1.5 once fruiting starts (high potassium) and supplement Cal-Mag at 0.5-1.0g/L to prevent blossom-end rot, B deficiency and Mg interveinal chlorosis under load.
Keep root-zone water 22-26C and dissolved oxygen >=5-6 mg/L; in PH lowland heat add an air stone or run frequent ebb cycles to avoid Pythium root rot in warm stagnant solution.

Provide 12-14h of strong light (PPFD ~400-600 umol/m2/s at canopy) and prune only lightly - train the main vine up and pinch lateral tips past the fruit; over-pruning cuts yield because most female flowers form on side shoots.

Hang yellow sticky traps plus cue-lure/methyl-eugenol pheromone traps early for melon fruit fly (Bactrocera cucurbitae); bag young fruit in paper/net sleeves 2-3 days after pollination to block stinging.
Manage downy and powdery mildew in humid PH air with 60-75% RH, strong canopy airflow, wide spacing, and rotate protectant sprays (potassium bicarbonate 5g/L, neem 3-5ml/L, copper) at first spotting.
Control aphids and whitefly (virus vectors) with neem oil 3% or imidacloprid 0.3ml/L and reflective mulch; remove and destroy any mosaic/leaf-curl plants quickly.

Harvest young and tender (10-18 days after pollination) while the rind is soft and your fingernail pierces it easily; tender upo is sweeter, and picking every 2-3 days keeps the vine setting new fruit for continuous harvest.
Flush the system with plain pH-5.8 water for 1-2 days between major nutrient resets and check EC daily during fruiting - a fruit-loaded upo drinks heavily and EC will creep up fast in hot weather.

risks.title: Trellis or peduncle failure under heavy fruit
risks.description: Upo vines are massive and fruits reach 0.6-1.2kg+; an undersized trellis or unsupported fruit tears the vine down or snaps the fruit stalk, killing the load.
risks.mitigation: Erect a 2-2.5m bamboo/GI-pipe frame rated 15-25kg per vine, train the main stem up, and cradle every fruit in a cloth/net sling tied to the frame before it gains weight.

risks.title: Poor fruit set from no pollination
risks.description: Flowers are white, open in the evening and are pollinated by night moths; indoors or in insect-screened setups no moths reach them, so female flowers wither and drop with no fruit.
risks.mitigation: Hand-pollinate every female in late afternoon/evening (anthesis 5-8pm): transfer pollen from a fresh male flower onto the female stigma the same evening or by early next morning to reach ~80% set.

risks.title: Melon fruit fly (Bactrocera cucurbitae)
risks.description: Adult flies sting young soft fruit and lay eggs; maggots tunnel inside, causing rot, deformity and premature fruit drop - the top cucurbit pest in PH lowlands.
risks.mitigation: Deploy cue-lure/methyl-eugenol pheromone and yellow sticky traps from first flowering, bag young fruit in paper/net sleeves, collect and destroy stung fruit, and field-sanitize fallen fruit daily.

risks.title: Downy and powdery mildew
risks.description: Humid PH conditions drive downy mildew (angular yellow leaf lesions) and powdery mildew (white coating); both defoliate the canopy and cut fruit sizing and yield.
risks.mitigation: Hold RH 60-75% with strong airflow and wide spacing, avoid wetting foliage, and rotate protectants - potassium bicarbonate 5g/L, neem 3-5ml/L, or copper - at the first lesions.

risks.title: Aphid/whitefly-borne virus
risks.description: Aphis gossypii and Bemisia tabaci transmit mosaic and leaf-curl viruses that stunt vines, mottle leaves and deform fruit; once infected, plants do not recover.
risks.mitigation: Scout undersides weekly, knock down vectors with neem oil 3% or imidacloprid 0.3ml/L, use reflective mulch and yellow traps, and rogue out symptomatic plants immediately.

risks.title: Blossom-end rot and Ca/Mg disorder under load
risks.description: Fast fruit growth on a heavy nutrient diet outpaces calcium/magnesium transport, giving sunken blossom-end rot, B-deficient cracking and interveinal leaf yellowing.
risks.mitigation: Hold fruiting EC at 2.2-2.5 (not higher), add Cal-Mag 0.5-1.0g/L weekly, keep root-zone moisture and water temp 22-26C steady, and maintain pH 5.8-6.2 for nutrient availability.

risks.title: Root rot from warm low-oxygen solution
risks.description: PH heat warms the nutrient solution; above ~28C dissolved oxygen drops and Pythium/root rot sets in, browning roots and wilting the vine despite full reservoirs.
risks.mitigation: Keep water 22-26C with shade and aeration, hold DO >=5-6 mg/L via air stones or frequent ebb cycles, and add beneficial microbes or H2O2 dosing if slime appears.

growth_stages.description: Soak hard upo seed overnight, then sow 1 seed per moist coco-coir plug ~15mm deep.
actions: Pre-soak seed 12-24h in lukewarm water to crack the hard coat
Sow into moist (not soggy) coco plug, pH 5.8

growth_stages.description: Cotyledons emerge in 3-7 days at warm temperatures.
actions: Keep 25-30C and high humidity until emergence
Begin dilute feed EC 1.2 once cotyledons open
Give 12h bright light to avoid stretching

growth_stages.description: First 2-3 true leaves develop; seedling readies for transplant.
actions: Transplant to 18-25L bucket when 3-4 true leaves / 10-15cm tall

growth_stages.description: Vine grows rapidly and is trained up the trellis; framework of laterals forms.
actions: Train main stem up 2-2.5m strong trellis, tie loosely
Space plants 80-120cm; keep water 22-26C with aeration
Pinch growing tip near top to push fruiting laterals

growth_stages.description: White evening flowers open; female flowers show a tiny gourd-shaped ovary at the base.
actions: Lift EC to 2.2, shift to higher-K feed (N:K ~1:1.5)
Hand-pollinate each female in late afternoon/evening (anthesis 5-8pm), or by early next morning
Add Cal-Mag 0.5-1.0g/L to prevent blossom-end rot
Hang fruit-fly pheromone and yellow sticky traps

growth_stages.description: Pollinated ovaries swell into young club-shaped fruit; load builds on the vine.
actions: Hold fruiting EC 2.2-2.5, pH 5.8-6.2, check EC daily
Sling/cradle each fruit to the trellis to protect the peduncle
Bag young fruit in net/paper to block fruit fly
Maintain DO >=5-6 mg/L and 60-75% RH with airflow

growth_stages.description: First tender fruit ready 10-18 days after pollination while skin is soft and green.
actions: Harvest when fingernail pierces rind easily, fruit 20-45cm / 600-1200g, not yellowing
Cut with 2-3cm stem using a sanitized blade
Pick every 2-3 days to trigger new flowering

growth_stages.description: Vine keeps flowering and fruiting for weeks; total ~5-8kg marketable per plant over the run.
actions: Keep hand-pollinating new females each evening
Hold EC 2.2-2.5 and replenish Cal-Mag weekly under load
Remove over-mature/yellow fruit promptly to sustain set
Stay on mildew and fruit-fly IPM through the run

common_issues: Cucurbit fruit fly (Bactrocera/Dacus) stinging young fruit and causing maggots/rot; downy and powdery mildew on dense humid canopy; aphids and whitefly vectoring cucumber/zucchini yellow mosaic virus; cucumber beetles and epilachna leaf beetle; poor fruit set and aborted ovaries from missed afternoon hand-pollination in screened setups; blossom-end rot and fruit cracking from Ca shortfall or EC/irrigation swings; root-zone hypoxia and Pythium root rot when water temp exceeds 28C; Mg/Fe interveinal chlorosis on heavy-feeding mature vines; over-mature fibrous fruit from delayed harvest; bitterness/toughness under heat and water stress.
harvest_indicator: Pick young and tender 7-12 days after fruit set: fruit 15-25cm long (smooth Luffa aegyptiaca up to 30cm), 2-4cm thick, bright green with crisp ridges, glossy taut skin that dents under a thumbnail and snaps cleanly. Skips toward loofah when skin dulls, hardens and seeds harden inside.
source: DA-ATI Region 7 & BusinessDiary PH Patola production guides, TheFoodGardener, GrowAI/Envirevo hydroponic cucumber CEA datasheets, FoodPulse ridge gourd, DA 2025 price monitoring
tips: Build a strong OVERHEAD trellis before transplant: vines run 6-9m and get heavy, so use a 2.0-2.2m high A-frame or horizontal net (bamboo/ipil-ipil or 2.5mm steel cable) rated for the fruit load; train one main stem up daily with soft ties.

Run in Dutch buckets (one vine per 11-18L bucket) or deep ebb-and-flow with coarse perlite or 60/40 cocopeat-perlite; space buckets 60-80cm (about 0.8-1.2 plants/m2) so the canopy gets airflow against mildew.
Ramp EC gradually: 1.2 mS/cm at seedling, 2.0 vegetative, then 2.4-2.8 once female flowers and fruit set; patola is a heavy feeder but EC above 3.0 in PH heat causes bitter, tough fruit.
Hold pH 5.8-6.2 for steady Ca/Mg/Fe uptake; check the bucket return daily because vigorous vines drink fast and drift the reservoir.
Keep K at or above N during fruiting (use a 1:0.5:1.5 N:P:K style bloom formula) and supplement Cal-Mag at about 0.5g/L weekly to prevent blossom-end rot and interveinal yellowing.

Patola is monoecious and ridge-type flowers open in late afternoon/evening, so HAND-POLLINATE between 4-7pm in screened houses: pick a fresh open male, strip petals, and dab pollen onto each receptive female stigma (or use a soft brush).
Encourage female flowers: lightly pinch lateral tips after the vine passes ~1.5m and avoid excess nitrogen, which pushes male flowers and leaf at the expense of fruit.
Target PPFD 500-700 umol/m2/s at canopy (DLI 20-25 mol/m2/day); supplement to 14-16h photoperiod in the wet season so fruit set does not stall under cloud.
Keep root-zone/water temperature 22-26C and never above 28C; maintain dissolved oxygen above 6 mg/L with air stones or frequent flood cycles to fend off Pythium in tropical heat.
Manage humidity 60-70% during fruiting with active airflow; trim shading lower leaves to expose fruit and break the leaf-wetness window that downy/powdery mildew exploit.
Hang cucurbit fruit-fly (methyl-eugenol) lures and yellow sticky traps from week 4, bag individual young fruit in paper/mesh sleeves, and rotate neem oil (5ml/L) or spinosad on a 7-day IPM cycle.
Harvest YOUNG and tender at 15-25cm every 2-3 days with a clean blade in early morning or late afternoon; frequent picking keeps the vine setting new fruit and stops any fruit going fibrous.
Let only chosen end-of-run fruit mature on the vine if you want loofah sponges; for eating, never let edible fruit pass the thumbnail-dent stage or it turns into a scratchy loofah core.

risks.title: Poor fruit set without hand-pollination
risks.description: Patola is monoecious and its ridge-type flowers open in the late afternoon/evening; in screened or indoor hydroponic houses there are few pollinators at that hour, so female flowers abort and drop without setting fruit.
risks.mitigation: Hand-pollinate daily between 4-7pm by transferring pollen from a fresh male flower (petals removed) onto each open female stigma; keep some male flowers on the vine and, if open, allow a few bees in during the flowering window.

risks.title: Over-mature fibrous fruit (loofah)
risks.description: If harvest is delayed even a few days the edible flesh develops tough fibers, the skin hardens and the fruit becomes the inedible loofah sponge, making it unmarketable as a vegetable.
risks.mitigation: Pick young and tender at 15-25cm (7-12 days after set) every 2-3 days; reserve only deliberately chosen end-of-season fruit on the vine if loofah sponges are wanted.

risks.title: Cucurbit fruit fly
risks.description: Bactrocera/Dacus females sting young fruit to lay eggs; maggots tunnel inside causing rot, deformity and premature drop, the single biggest field pest of luffa in PH lowlands.
risks.mitigation: Deploy methyl-eugenol lure traps from week 4, bag individual young fruit in paper/mesh sleeves, remove and destroy stung fruit, and spot-spray spinosad or neem oil on a 7-day cycle.

risks.title: Downy and powdery mildew
risks.description: Dense vining canopy plus 80%+ humidity and leaf wetness in the wet season drive downy (yellow angular leaf spots) and powdery (white surface bloom) mildew, defoliating vines and cutting yield.
risks.mitigation: Hold fruiting humidity 60-70%, run constant airflow, prune lower/shading leaves for an open canopy, and rotate potassium-bicarbonate, sulfur or copper sprays preventively in rainy months.

risks.title: Trellis collapse / vine load failure
risks.description: Vines reach 6-9m and carry heavy fruit; an undersized or low trellis sags, snaps stems, lets fruit touch wet media and rot, and crowds the canopy into a mildew trap.
risks.mitigation: Install a 2.0-2.2m overhead A-frame or net trellis rated for the load before transplant, train one main stem daily with soft ties, and support heavy individual fruit with mesh slings.

risks.title: Blossom-end rot and fruit cracking
risks.description: Calcium shortfall, EC spikes above 3.0 mS/cm, or swinging irrigation in PH heat cause sunken dark blossom-end lesions and skin cracking on rapidly expanding young fruit.
risks.mitigation: Hold EC 2.4-2.8 during fruiting, keep pH 5.8-6.2, add Cal-Mag ~0.5g/L weekly, and keep media moisture even with frequent short fertigation cycles rather than large swings.

risks.title: Root-zone hypoxia and Pythium root rot
risks.description: Tropical reservoirs warming past 28C lose dissolved oxygen; roots suffocate and Pythium invades, wilting heavy-transpiring vines even when the solution is full.
risks.mitigation: Shade and insulate tanks, keep water 22-26C, maintain DO above 6 mg/L with air stones or frequent flood-drain cycles, and sanitize lines between crops.

growth_stages.description: Sow 1 scarified seed per rockwool/coco plug; ridge-luffa seed germinates in 4-10 days and benefits from a warm soak.
actions: Pre-soak seed 12-24h in warm water and nick the hard seed coat
Set rockwool to pH 5.8, hold media 26-30C, plain water or EC <=0.8
Keep humidity 70-80% under a dome until the radicle and cotyledons emerge

growth_stages.description: First true leaves and a leading tendril form; the young vine starts reaching for support.
actions: Feed dilute solution at EC 1.2 mS/cm, pH 5.8-6.2
Set a stake or string the seedling will grab as the tendril appears

growth_stages.description: Vigorous true-leaf plant moved to its final Dutch bucket / ebb-and-flow position and tied to the overhead trellis.
actions: Transplant to 11-18L coarse-perlite or coco-perlite buckets at 60-80cm spacing (about 0.8-1.2 plants/m2)
Raise EC toward 1.8-2.0 mS/cm, keep root-zone 22-26C
Begin daily training of one main stem up the 2.0-2.2m trellis

growth_stages.description: Rapid vine extension up the trellis with abundant leaf and tendril; the framework for fruiting is laid down.
actions: Hold EC 2.0 mS/cm, pH 5.8-6.2, with balanced N:K
Train and tie the main stem daily; remove weak lower laterals for airflow
Lightly pinch lateral tips after ~1.5m to encourage female-flower nodes

growth_stages.description: Clustered male flowers appear first at lower nodes, then solitary female flowers (tiny fruit at the base) open in the late afternoon/evening.
actions: Shift to bloom formula, K >= N, EC 2.4-2.6 mS/cm, add Cal-Mag ~0.5g/L weekly
Hand-pollinate daily 4-7pm: dab a fresh male onto each open female stigma
Hang methyl-eugenol fruit-fly lures and yellow sticky traps

growth_stages.description: Pollinated ovaries swell rapidly into ridged young fruit; calcium and potassium demand peak.
actions: Hold EC 2.6-2.8 mS/cm, pH 5.8-6.2; keep DO >6 mg/L and water 22-26C
Maintain humidity 60-70% with active airflow against mildew
Bag/sleeve young fruit and support heavy fruit with mesh slings

growth_stages.description: Earliest fruit reach edible size; pick young and tender before any fiber develops.
actions: Harvest at 15-25cm, 2-4cm thick, when skin dents under a thumbnail
Cut with a clean blade in early morning or late afternoon
Cool harvested fruit promptly and grade out any fruit-fly-stung fruit

growth_stages.description: Vine keeps flowering and setting for 6-10 weeks; frequent picking sustains yield of ~2.5-3.5kg per plant over the run.
actions: Pick every 2-3 days so no fruit turns fibrous; keep up afternoon hand-pollination
Maintain EC 2.6-2.8 mS/cm and weekly Cal-Mag; top up reservoir daily
Continue IPM (neem/spinosad 7-day rotation) and remove spent/diseased leaves

common_issues: Calcium-linked tip/margin burn on new leaves at EC >2.5 or low humidity; bolting and bitter, tough leaves under sustained day temps >27C in PH lowlands; aphids (cabbage/green peach) colonizing curled leaf folds; cabbage looper, diamondback moth, and imported cabbage worm chewing foliage; whitefly and flea beetle on undersides; downy mildew (yellow angular patches, gray sporulation) in humid wet-season air; Alternaria/black leaf spot (dark target rings) and powdery mildew; black rot (V-shaped marginal yellowing, blackened veins) from infected seed/splash; bottom/crown rot in saturated media; N or Mg interveinal chlorosis on older leaves at low EC
harvest_indicator: Outer leaves 20-30cm long, deep blue-green, fully ruffled and firm but not leathery; first cut-and-come-again harvest around day 50-60 once 8-12 mature leaves have formed; pick lower/outer leaves at 30-50g each, leaving the central growing crown and 6-8 inner leaves intact
source: GrowAI kale hydroponic guide, UpStart University CEA kale guide, NatureTech/NThydroponics kale guide, Cornell & UMN Extension brassica disease factsheets, PSA/DA PH price monitoring
tips: Sow 1-2 seeds per rockwool/phenolic plug 5-6mm deep; curly types like 'Winterbor', 'Darkibor', and 'Vates' germinate in 4-7 days at 18-24C root zone.
Run a stepped EC ramp: 0.8-1.0 at germination, 1.4 for seedlings, 1.8-2.0 in early vegetative, then hold 2.0-2.2 (max 2.5) at maturity; kale is a hungrier feeder than lettuce.
Keep pH 5.8-6.2 for best Ca/Mg/Fe uptake; curly kale shows Fe chlorosis on new growth if pH drifts above 6.5.
Final spacing 25-30cm head-to-head in NFT/DFT (about 9-12 plants/m2) so the broad ruffled canopy gets airflow and light to lower leaves.
Target PPFD 200-300 umol/m2/s for seedlings rising to 400-600 for mature plants, photoperiod 14-16h, DLI 14-17 mol/m2/day.
In DWC/Kratky keep dissolved oxygen above 6-7 mg/L and reservoir 18-22C; warm stagnant water above 24C invites crown/root rot in this brassica.
Harvest cut-and-come-again: snap 2-4 outer leaves every 7-14 days, never removing more than one-third of leaf mass, and always leave the apical crown so the plant keeps producing for 8-12 weeks.
In PH lowland heat, hang 30-40% shade cloth and chill the reservoir to 18-20C from day 35 to delay bolting and keep leaves sweet rather than bitter.
Supplement Cal-Mag (~0.5g/L weekly) and keep humidity 50-70% with gentle canopy airflow to prevent calcium-deficiency tip burn during fast growth.
Scout the curled leaf folds twice weekly for aphids; knock down with insecticidal soap or 1-2% neem, and release ladybird/lacewing predators in screened houses.
Exclude diamondback moth, cabbage looper, and imported cabbage worm with 0.4mm insect netting; spot-treat caterpillars with Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) at label rate.
In the wet season, increase air movement and lower humidity to suppress downy mildew, Alternaria black spot, and powdery mildew; remove and bag infected leaves promptly.
Use certified hot-water-treated seed and sanitize channels between crops to avoid seed-borne black rot (Xanthomonas) spreading through recirculating solution.
Cool-season crop: best PH planting window is the cooler, drier Dec-Feb months in the lowlands, or year-round in the highlands (Benguet) and screened/cooled greenhouses.

risks.title: Calcium tip burn
risks.description: New inner leaf margins brown and crisp as transpiration fails to pull enough Ca to fast-growing tissue, worsened by EC >2.5, low humidity, or weak airflow.
risks.mitigation: Hold EC at 2.0-2.2, keep humidity 50-70%, run constant gentle airflow over the canopy, and add Cal-Mag ~0.5g/L weekly.

risks.title: Bolting and bitterness in tropical heat
risks.description: Sustained PH lowland day temps above 27C and night temps above 20C push curly kale to elongate a flower stalk and turn leaves tough and bitter, ending useful harvest.
risks.mitigation: Use 30-40% shade cloth, chill the reservoir to 18-20C, harvest leaves frequently, and time crops to the cool Dec-Feb window or grow in the highlands/cooled greenhouses.

risks.title: Aphid infestation
risks.description: Cabbage and green peach aphids hide and breed in the tight curls of the leaf, causing distortion, honeydew, sooty mold, and virus spread that goes unnoticed until heavy.
risks.mitigation: Scout leaf folds twice weekly, spray insecticidal soap or 1-2% neem to the undersides, release ladybirds/lacewings, and screen vents with fine netting.

risks.title: Caterpillar pests (loopers, diamondback moth, cabbage worm)
risks.description: Lepidopteran larvae skeletonize and hole the leaves; diamondback moth is especially persistent and pesticide-resistant in PH brassica plots.
risks.mitigation: Exclude adults with 0.4mm insect netting, hand-pick larvae, and apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) or spinosad at label rate, rotating modes of action.

risks.title: Downy and powdery mildew
risks.description: High wet-season humidity and poor airflow trigger downy mildew (yellow angular patches with gray underside sporulation) and powdery mildew (white surface coating) on the dense canopy.
risks.mitigation: Widen spacing to 25-30cm, force airflow, keep humidity below 70%, remove infected leaves, and apply a labeled biofungicide (e.g. Bacillus/potassium bicarbonate) preventively.

risks.title: Black rot and Alternaria leaf spot
risks.description: Seed-borne Xanthomonas black rot (V-shaped marginal yellowing with blackened veins) and Alternaria/black spot (dark concentric target rings) cut leaf marketability and can collapse plants.
risks.mitigation: Plant certified hot-water-treated seed, avoid leaf wetting and overhead splash, sanitize channels and tools between crops, and rogue out symptomatic plants early.

risks.title: Root/crown rot in warm water
risks.description: Reservoir or root-zone temps above 24C with low dissolved oxygen invite Pythium/crown rot, browning roots and wilting the dense kale canopy.
risks.mitigation: Keep water 18-22C (chill if needed), maintain DO above 6-7 mg/L with strong aeration, and avoid waterlogged, oxygen-poor media around the crown.

growth_stages.description: Sow curly kale seed in rockwool/phenolic plugs; radicle and cotyledons emerge in 4-7 days.
actions: Sow 1-2 seeds 5-6mm deep per plug pre-soaked to pH 5.8
Keep plugs moist and lightly covered until emergence

growth_stages.description: True leaves form; plants build a fibrous root mass ready for the channel.
actions: Thin to one strong seedling per plug

growth_stages.description: Transplant into NFT/DFT/DWC at 3-4 true leaves; roots establish in the nutrient film or solution.
actions: Raise EC to 1.6-1.8, keep water 18-22C, DO >6 mg/L
Shade to 30-40% during midday in hot weather

growth_stages.description: Rapid leaf expansion; the ruffled rosette and frame of the plant develop.
actions: Add Cal-Mag ~0.5g/L weekly to pre-empt tip burn
Begin twice-weekly scouting for aphids and caterpillars; net vents

growth_stages.description: Plant reaches harvest frame size with 8-12 large mature leaves.
actions: Hold EC 2.0-2.2 (cap 2.5), maintain airflow and humidity 50-70%
Chill reservoir to 18-20C and keep shade cloth up to delay bolting
Apply Bt/neem as needed on a rotation for pest pressure

growth_stages.description: First cut-and-come-again harvest of outer leaves once 8-12 mature 20-30cm leaves have formed.
actions: Snap 2-4 outer leaves at 30-50g each in early morning
Leave the central crown and 6-8 inner leaves intact
Cool harvested leaves to 4C within an hour for shelf life

growth_stages.description: Repeat outer-leaf harvests every 7-14 days for 8-12 weeks, accumulating 200-400g per plant.
actions: Remove no more than one-third of leaf mass per harvest
Top up nutrients to EC 2.0-2.2 and monitor for Ca tip burn
Retire plants once bolting, mildew, or pest damage degrades leaf quality

common_issues: Cercospora/Colletotrichum leaf spot in stagnant humid air; aphids and whiteflies on tender shoot tips; bitter, tough leaves under water stress or insufficient N; nitrate and oxalate accumulation when over-fed (EC too high or excess nitrate-N); premature flowering/seeding under short days or moisture stress slows leaf output; root rot (Pythium) if water temp exceeds 30C with low DO; leaf-miner trails on older leaves; Mg deficiency interveinal yellowing on lower leaves at high EC; root-knot nematodes possible if recycled media reused.
harvest_indicator: First cut when vines reach 30-45cm with several pairs of thick, glossy, succulent leaves (leaf blade 8-12cm); harvest 15-20cm tender shoot tips with 4-6 expanded leaves; stems still soft and snap cleanly; pick before stems lignify or flower spikes appear.
source: UF/IFAS HS1371 Malabar Spinach cultivation guide, LaNiTex Red Malabar hydroponic guide, JuanMagsasaka/Business Diary PH alugbati production guides, oxalatecontent.com nutrient data
tips: Alugbati is a true heat-lover: it thrives at 26-34C lowland PH conditions where Western true spinach bolts and dies above 24C, making it the better hot-season leafy green for the Philippines.

It is NOT a true spinach but a fast-growing twining vine (Basella alba green; B. rubra red-stemmed) needing a light vertical or V-shaped trellis, net, or grow-tube to climb 1.5-2m and keep leaves clean and air-circulated.

Propagate fast from 20-25cm tip cuttings: strip lower leaves, hold in a dark damp space 1-2 days to callus, then set directly in net pots filled with hydroton/rockwool - roots emerge in 5-10 days and skip the seed stage entirely.
For seed, scarify the hard coat with sandpaper and pre-soak 24h; germinate plugs at 24-30C, expecting 10-21 days - much slower than cuttings.
Ramp EC gradually: 0.8 mS/cm seedling, 1.6 mS/cm vegetative, up to 2.2-2.4 mS/cm at full cut-and-come-again production; do not exceed 2.4 or leaves accumulate nitrate and taste mealy.
Hold pH 5.8-6.5; this vine tolerates a wide range but 6.0-6.3 gives the best Fe/Mn/Mg uptake for deep-green succulent foliage.
Spacing: run 25-30cm plant-to-plant in DWC/NFT, about 4-6 plants/m2 of channel, because each plant fans out into a sprawling vine - tighter spacing invites leaf spot.
Keep root-zone water 22-28C and dissolved oxygen above 5 mg/L; above 30C with low DO, Pythium root rot sets in fast in tropical reservoirs - add an air stone or shade/insulate the tank.
Run cut-and-come-again: first harvest the growing tips at day 35-50, then re-cut 15-20cm shoots every 7-14 days; each cut behind a node forces 2 new laterals, so yield climbs over a 3-6 month run.
Give 8-12h direct sun or 12-16h full-spectrum LED at 250-400 PPFD; light afternoon shade (30% cloth) in peak PH summer keeps leaves larger, more succulent and less bitter.
Favor a balanced nitrate-to-ammonium leafy formula but keep total N moderate - excess nitrate-N drives nitrate/oxalate buildup and rank flavor; alugbati is already a moderate-oxalate green.
For aphids/whiteflies on tender tips, use yellow sticky traps plus insecticidal soap or 1-2% neem oil weekly; the plant is otherwise quite pest-tolerant compared to lettuce.
Harvest in the cool early morning when shoots are fully turgid; the thick mucilaginous leaves wilt fast, so move to 10-12C cooling within an hour and use within 2-3 days.
Pinch out emerging flower spikes promptly - flowering diverts energy from leaves and the messy purple-black berries (B. rubra) stain and reduce marketable shoot quality.

risks.title: Leaf spot (Cercospora / Colletotrichum)
risks.description: Fungal leaf spots cause brown-to-black lesions on the thick leaves, worst in stagnant, very humid canopies common in tropical screen houses; the dense sprawling vine traps moisture.
risks.mitigation: Trellis vertically for airflow, space 25-30cm, keep humidity 60-75% with active fans, remove and destroy infected leaves immediately, and avoid wetting foliage; rotate to a copper or biofungicide (Bacillus subtilis) at first sign.

risks.title: Nitrate and oxalate accumulation
risks.description: As a fast leafy crop, alugbati readily stores nitrate and oxalic acid when fed too much nitrogen or run at high EC, lowering food quality and adding a metallic/oxalate taste.
risks.mitigation: Cap EC at 2.2-2.4 mS/cm, use a balanced leafy formula with moderate nitrate-N, never over-dose N, and consider a 2-3 day clear-water/low-EC flush before a big harvest to lower leaf nitrate.

risks.title: Pythium root rot in warm reservoirs
risks.description: PH lowland water temperatures easily exceed 30C, and warm water holds little oxygen; alugbati's vigorous roots in DWC then rot, leaves yellow and the vine collapses.
risks.mitigation: Keep reservoir 22-28C (shade, insulate, or chill the tank), maintain DO above 5 mg/L with air stones, add beneficial microbes (Trichoderma/Bacillus), and sanitize between crop cycles.

risks.title: Aphids and whiteflies on shoot tips
risks.description: Soft, succulent growing tips attract aphids and whiteflies that suck sap, deform new leaves, and spread sooty mold and virus; populations explode in warm enclosed setups.
risks.mitigation: Scout twice weekly, hang yellow sticky traps, spray insecticidal soap or 1-2% neem on undersides of tips, and release ladybugs/lacewings; screen vents at 50-mesh in protected structures.

risks.title: Bitter, tough leaves from water/heat stress
risks.description: Inconsistent feeding, low moisture in Kratky as the level drops, or extreme heat with full sun makes leaves bitter, fibrous and triggers premature flowering, cutting leaf yield.
risks.mitigation: Keep the root zone continuously moist (top up Kratky reservoirs, never let DWC run dry), provide 30% afternoon shade in peak summer, and harvest young tips frequently to keep growth tender.

risks.title: Premature flowering / seeding
risks.description: Short days, moisture stress, or aging vines push alugbati into flowering and berry set, which halts new leaf production and lowers marketable shoot quality.
